EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough moved through southern California late on the 23rd into the morning of the 24th, providing another round of light to moderate precipitation, and strong winds in the mountains and desert slopes. Many locations gusted over 50 mph during the morning and early afternoon of the 24th. Another, deeper, trough dug south through the region and on the 26th into the morning of the 27th, bringing more strong, gusty winds to the mountains and desert slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Volcan Mountain SDGE mesonet between 0850 and 0900 PST on the 24th and ended between 1320 and 1330 PST the same day. During this time winds mostly gusted over 60 mph, with a peak gust of 70 mph between 1110 and 1120 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough moved through southern California late on the 23rd into the morning of the 24th, providing another round of light to moderate precipitation, and strong winds in the mountains and desert slopes. Many locations gusted over 50 mph during the morning and early afternoon of the 24th. Another, deeper, trough dug south through the region and on the 26th into the morning of the 27th, bringing more strong, gusty winds to the mountains and desert slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Burns Canyon RAWS between 0651 and 1050 PST on the 24th. During this time, a peak gust of 62 mph was measured between 0651 and 0751 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough moved through southern California late on the 23rd into the morning of the 24th, providing another round of light to moderate precipitation, and strong winds in the mountains and desert slopes. Many locations gusted over 50 mph during the morning and early afternoon of the 24th. Another, deeper, trough dug south through the region and on the 26th into the morning of the 27th, bringing more strong, gusty winds to the mountains and desert slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted over 50 mph between 1200 and 1900 PST on the 26th. During this time, peak wind gusts of 59 mph and 64 mph occurred around 1700 and 1800 PST, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, cold low pressure remained over the northeast Pacific from the 21st through early on the 24th bringing heavy snow to eastern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A solid 1 to 2 feet of snow fell near and west of highway 395 north of Janesville with 3 or more feet in the Feather River Basin along highways 70 and 89 from Portola north and west.  At the Grizzly Ridge HADS (12 miles WNW Portola at 6900 feet), 7.5 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation fell which likely translates to at least 5 to 7 feet of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and fast-moving system moved across the northern Pacific and into California on the 26th. The system brought a relatively quick period of heavy snow to the northern Sierra from the afternoon of the 25th through the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread 8 to 13 inches of snow fell in the Lake Tahoe Basin east of highway 89 with this system. For areas along and west of highway 89, 15 to 20 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and fast-moving system moved across the northern Pacific and into California on the 26th. The system brought a relatively quick period of heavy snow to the northern Sierra from the afternoon of the 25th through the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along and east of highway 395, 4 to 8 inches (locally to 9 inches) of snow fell with this system. For areas west of highway 395, one to 2 feet of snow fell with local amounts up to 29 inches near Mammoth Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, cold low pressure remained over the northeast Pacific from the 21st through early on the 24th bringing heavy snow to eastern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread two to three feet of snow fell from midday on the 21th through late evening on the 23rd in the Lake Tahoe area, with 4 to 5 feet near the crest and at higher elevations. In addition to heavy snow, winds gusted between 35 and 45 mph at times for periods of significantly reduced visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, cold low pressure remained over the northeast Pacific from the 21st through early on the 24th bringing heavy snow to eastern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to 4 feet of snow, highest at the immediate Sierra crest, fell west of highway 395 from the evening of the 21st through early morning on the 24th. Near and east of highway 395, 1 to 2 feet of snow fell as far south as Mammoth Lakes (only 9 inches at Crowley Lake). In addition to heavy snow, winds gusted to between 35 and 50 mph at times along highway 395.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large west to northwest swell generated high surf on the 1st and 2nd, mainly along west-facing beaches of Orange and San Diego County. Surf heights peaked on the 1st and gradually decreased into the 3rd. Tidal swings of over 6 feet occurred between the highest morning high tides and lowest afternoon low tides. The highest surf occurred in San Diego County with heights averaging 5-8 feet and sets to 13 feet at times. No damage was observed, though the Ocean Beach Pier was closed for a time due to the high surf.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Huntington Beach lifeguards reported surf of 7 to 8 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system and associated cold front brought isolated heavy snow and locally high wind to the southern Great Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 395 was closed in the Owens Valley due to poor visibility in blowing dust.  The peak measured gust was 79 mph 4 miles NW of Independence.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds developed across the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH were reported in many areas with the strongest winds through the Interstate 5 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds developed in the mountains of Los Angeles county. Some wind gust reports from local RAWS sensors: Whitaker Peak (Gusts 58-67 MPH), Camp Nine (Gusts 63-67 MPH) and Chilao (Gusts 62-64 MPH).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies, high humidity just inland from the coast, along with the radiational cooling allowed dense fog to form near the coast during the nights and early mornings of the 23rd and 25th. Drainage flow kept the fog within about 10 miles of the coast, with the exception of the inland, low area of Ramona. Multiple airports reported visibilities less than 1/4 mile for several hours on each of those mornings. The fog created some airport delays as well as security problems for the Border Patrol along the Mexican Border and along the coast up to San Clemente.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Carlsbad-Palomar Airport ASOS reported visibility of one-quarter mile or less from 2141 PST on the 24th to 1000 PST on the 25th. Brown Field ASOS reported visibility of one-quarter mile or less from 2053 PST on the 24th to 0921 PST on the 25th, except between 0123 and 0153 when the visibility briefly improved. Miramar Airport ASOS reported visibility of one-quarter mile or less from 2355 PST on the 24th to 0855 PST on the 25th, down to one-sixteenth of a mile between 0055 and 0736 PST on the 25th. Montgomery Field ASOS reported visibility of one-quarter mile or less from 1253 to 0838 PST on the 25th. A spotter in Allied Gardens reported visibility less than one-quarter mile around 0212 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, Pacific low pressure system generated a large WNW swell of 8 to 10 feet around 14 seconds. This produced high surf conditions along the west-facing San Diego and Orange County beaches. Average surf heights were 5-8 feet with sets to 12 feet in some areas. This surf contributed to severe beach erosion at Moonlight Beach in Encinitas. Erosion of up to 4 feet has erased a large portion of the sand replacement project previously in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Huntington Beach lifeguards reported surf up to 10 feet this day.

A series of winter storms kept interior Northern California wet for most of a week.  It was the first notable winter storms in the region since October 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A relatively weak winter storm brought 3-6 inches of snow to elevations above 1500 feet and one to two feet of snow to elevations above 3000 feet.  The heaviest snow fell on the morning of Thursday, January 19th and impacted Hwy 299, I-5, and Shasta Lake.  Winds were light, with local gusts only to 30 mph.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The highest King Tides of the winter season impacted the Bay Area on December 13 and 14. Minor coastal flooding during the times of high tide was observed at many areas with some minor damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streets in Mill Valley were flooded at the time of high tide during the King Tide.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, long period swell impacted the California coastline for two days from December 28-29. A pair of fishermen were killed during the event from a sneaker wave which washed them off the rocks they were fishing on.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pair of fishermen, father and son, and a friend were fishing on rocks near the Golden Gate Bridge. A large sneaker wave swept them off the rocks and into the ocean. The friend was able to swim back to shore and contacted authorities. A Tiburon Fire Department boat found the bodies of the father and son about 100 yards offshore with the assistance of a Coast Guard Helicopter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and cold storm brought heavy snow and strong winds to Northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 4N McCloud in zone CAZ082 immediately adjacent to CAZ083 reported 17.0 inches in 24 hours ending at 19/1500 PST.  Given that this well exceeds the warning criteria, it is assumed that snow meeting warning criteria fell in parts of CAZ083.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of gusty northwest to north winds affected the mountains and deserts of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Numerous reports of sustained winds in excess of 40 MPH and gusts in excess of 60 MPH were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong west to northwest winds were reported across the Antelope Valley. The RAWS sensor at Lake Palmdale reported a wind gust of 62 MPH while the ASOS sensors at Lancaster and Palmdale reported sustained winds between 40 and 43 MPH.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of gusty northwest to north winds affected the mountains and deserts of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Numerous reports of sustained winds in excess of 40 MPH and gusts in excess of 60 MPH were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest to north winds developed across the mountains of Los Angeles county. The RAWS sensor at Camp 9 reported wind gusts between 58 and 62 MPH while the Chilao RAWS sensor reported gusts between 62 and 76 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, Pacific low pressure system generated a large WNW swell of 8 to 10 feet around 14 seconds. This produced high surf conditions along the west-facing San Diego and Orange County beaches. Average surf heights were 5-8 feet with sets to 12 feet in some areas. This surf contributed to severe beach erosion at Moonlight Beach in Encinitas. Erosion of up to 4 feet has erased a large portion of the sand replacement project previously in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lifeguards at Solana Beach reported surf of 7 feet with max sets to 10 feet. An NWS employee reported surf of 6 to 8 feet with occasional 12 foot sets at Sunset Cliffs. Surfline.com reported surf of 5 to 7 feet with occasional sets to 9 feet at Blacks Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms kept interior Northern California wet for most of a week.  It was the first notable winter storms in the region since October 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A relatively weak winter storm brought six to twelve inches of snow to elevations between 5000 and 7000 feet and up to two feet of snow to elevations above 7000 feet, including 14 inches to Lassen Volcanic National Park.  Winds were 20-35 mph sustained with gusts 50-70 mph over ridgetops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms kept interior Northern California wet for most of a week.  It was the first notable winter storms in the region since October 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A moderate winter storm brought up to a foot of snow to elevations above 3500 feet.  High winds made visibilities poor in some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms kept interior Northern California wet for most of a week.  It was the first notable winter storms in the region since October 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A moderate winter storm brought up to a foot of snow to elevations above 3500 feet.  High winds made visibilities poor in some locations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A moderate winter storm brought up to a foot of snow to elevations above 3500 feet.  High winds made visibilities poor in some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms kept interior Northern California wet for most of a week.  It was the first notable winter storms in the region since October 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A moderate winter storm brought six to eight inches of snow down to 4500 feet and eighteen inches of snow to elevations above 6500 feet.  Winds gusted to 30-40 mph along the ridgetops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms kept interior Northern California wet for most of a week.  It was the first notable winter storms in the region since October 2011.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A moderate winter storm brought up to a foot of snow down to 4000 feet and up to two feet of snow to elevations above 7000 feet.  Winds gusted to 50 mph along the ridgetops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third in a series of strong storms brought high winds to the Shasta Valley in Northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CALTRANS sensor at Weed Airport recorded a gust to 62 mph at 22/1655 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of gusty northwest to north winds affected the mountains and deserts of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Numerous reports of sustained winds in excess of 40 MPH and gusts in excess of 60 MPH were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest to north winds developed across the mountains of Ventura county. Observations across the area indicated wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late-season winter storm brought significant precipitation and gusty winds to Southwestern California. Rainfall totals generally ranged between 1 and 4 inches. This heavy rainfall produced widespread reports of urban flooding. In the mountains, the resorts (above 6000 feet) received significant snowfall, ranging anywhere from 9 to 22 inches. The snowfall combined with gusty south winds to produce dangerous winter storm conditions in the local mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful late-season storm generated strong southwesterly winds across the Antelope Valley. The ASOS at Palmdale reported sustained winds of 43 MPH.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late-season winter storm brought significant precipitation and gusty winds to Southwestern California. Rainfall totals generally ranged between 1 and 4 inches. This heavy rainfall produced widespread reports of urban flooding. In the mountains, the resorts (above 6000 feet) received significant snowfall, ranging anywhere from 9 to 22 inches. The snowfall combined with gusty south winds to produce dangerous winter storm conditions in the local mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful late-season storm produced dangerous winter weather conditions across the mountains of Los Angeles county. Above 6000 feet, significant snowfall was reported. Some snow reports from the local resorts included: Mountain High (18-22 inches), Mount Baldy (18 inches), Big Pines (11 inches), and Wrightwood (9 inches). Along with the heavy snowfall, gusty south winds in excess of 65 MPH were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late-season winter storm brought significant precipitation and gusty winds to Southwestern California. Rainfall totals generally ranged between 1 and 4 inches. This heavy rainfall produced widespread reports of urban flooding. In the mountains, the resorts (above 6000 feet) received significant snowfall, ranging anywhere from 9 to 22 inches. The snowfall combined with gusty south winds to produce dangerous winter storm conditions in the local mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful late-season storm produced dangerous winter weather conditions across the mountains of Ventura county. Above 6000 feet, significant snowfall (12 to 20 inches) was reported. Along with the heavy snowfall, gusty south winds in excess of 65 MPH were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming cold front brought high winds to the Shasta Valley and areas east of the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CALTRANS sensor in North Weed recorded a gust to 58 mph at 28/1935 and a gust to 60 mph at 28/1950 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought a brief period of high winds to the Shasta Valley near Weed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CALTRANS sensor at Weed reported a gust to 63 mph at 25/1310 PST. The Weed RAWS reported a gust to 62 mph at 25/1448 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In early January, the first significant Santa Ana wind event developed across the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles Counties. North to northeast wind gusts between 58 and 83 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Topanga RAWS sensor reported north to northeast wind gusts of 63 MPH.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In early January, the first significant Santa Ana wind event developed across the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles Counties. North to northeast wind gusts between 58 and 83 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty north to northeast wind developed across the mountains of Los Angeles County. The Camp Nine RAWS sensor reported the highest gusts between 58 and 83 MPH. Other RAWS sensors with significant wind gusts included: Chilao (59 to 69 MPH), Mill Creek (58 to 65 MPH), and Warm Springs (63 to 67 MPH).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high winds to the Shasta Valley on this date.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CALTRANS sensor at North Weed recorded gusts to 58 mph at 31/0400 PST and 31/0445 PST.  The CALTRANS sensor at Weed Airport recorded a gust to 58 mph at 31/0510 PST.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second late-season storm brought another round of rain, wind and snow to Southwestern California. Rainfall totals across the area generally ranged between 1 and 3 inches which resulted in numerous reports of urban flooding. In the mountains, significant snowfall was reported with areas above 5000 feet receiving over 10 inches. The heavy snowfall combined with strong southerly winds to produce dangerous winter weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A late-season winter storm brought dangerous winter weather conditions to the mountains of Los Angeles county. Above 5000 feet, significant snowfall occurred. Some reports include: Mountain High (14 inches) and Wrightwood (14 inches). The heavy snowfall combined with gusty south winds in excess of 60 MPH to produce dangerous winter storm conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second late-season storm brought another round of rain, wind and snow to Southwestern California. Rainfall totals across the area generally ranged between 1 and 3 inches which resulted in numerous reports of urban flooding. In the mountains, significant snowfall was reported with areas above 5000 feet receiving over 10 inches. The heavy snowfall combined with strong southerly winds to produce dangerous winter weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A late-season winter storm brought dangerous winter weather conditions to the mountains of Ventura county. Above 5000 feet, significant snowfall occurred. Some reports include: Mount Pinos (12 inches), Pine Mountain Club (8 inches), and Frazier Park (10 inches). The heavy snowfall combined with gusty south winds in excess of 60 MPH to produce dangerous winter storm conditions.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The highest King Tides of the winter season impacted the Bay Area on December 13 and 14. Minor coastal flooding during the times of high tide was observed at many areas with some minor damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor coastal flooding in Marin County at the time of the King Tide caused Highway 1 to be closed at the time of high tide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds developed across the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH were reported in many areas with the strongest winds through the Interstate 5 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds developed in the mountains of Ventura county. Wind gusts between 60 and 65 MPH were reported with the strongest winds near the Interstate 5 corridor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In early January, the first significant Santa Ana wind event developed across the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles Counties. North to northeast wind gusts between 58 and 83 MPH were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty north to northeast wind developed across the mountains of Ventura County. Wind gusts in excess of 75 MPH were reported in some locations.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure over the Great Basin generated another round of strong Santa Ana winds across Southern California. The strongest winds occurred in the mountains and valleys of Ventura and Los Angeles counties with wind gusts as high as 70 MPH reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northeast winds were reported across the mountains of Los Angeles county. The Camp Nine RAWS sensor reported a peak wind gust of 69 MPH. Other strong wind reports included the Clear Creek RAWS (gust to 68 MPH) and the Chilao RAWS (gust to 59 MPH).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure over the Great Basin generated another round of strong Santa Ana winds across Southern California. The strongest winds occurred in the mountains and valleys of Ventura and Los Angeles counties with wind gusts as high as 70 MPH reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northeast winds were reported across the mountains of Ventura county. Wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The next upper level low arrived to the region on the night of March 27th-28th after a brief dry period between storm systems. This storm stayed mainly over northern California, and brought only light precipitation to the region. A stronger storm arrived on the last day of the month, bringing heavier precipitation to the region. Rainfall amounts for March 31st-April 1st were between a quarter and a half inch of rain for the central San Joaquin Valley, and between a tenth and a quarter of an inch for the south Valley. The exception was the southwest part of the San Joaquin Valley, which was rain-shadowed and received only a few hundredths of an inch of rain. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, snowfall amounts over the high country generally were between 5 and 9 inches, although a couple of sites in Tulare County had around a foot of new snow. Gusty southeast winds developed ahead of the cold front, with gusts of 35 to 45 mph in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley, and 58 to 62 mph in the Kern County deserts. A large tree branch fell on an SUV in southwest Bakersfield.||The above-normal rainfall for March (except at Merced) helped put a dent in the rainfall deficit for the 2011-2012 season. When March began, Bakersfield was at 45.5 percent of normal, and Fresno was at 47.0 percent. At the end of March, Bakersfield had improved to 58.2 percent of normal, and Fresno was at 61.9 percent of normal.||(See the table on the next page.)||THE 2011-2012 RAIN SEASON THROUGH MARCH 31ST|/AMOUNTS IN INCHES/|SITE MAR NRML DEP PCT SEASON NORMAL DEP PCT|2012 MAR* NRML 3/31 3/31* NRML|MERCED AIRPORT 2.02 2.07 -0.05 97.6 5.33 10.84 -5.51 49.2|MERCED CITY 1.89 2.07 -0.18 91.3 5.66 11.48 -5.82 49.3|MADERA AIRPORT 3.23 1.80 1.43 179.4 6.28 10.40 -4.12 60.4|FRESNO 2.43 2.03 0.40 119.7 6.13 9.91 -3.78 61.9|HANFORD AIRPORT 1.72 1.63 0.09 105.5 4.00 8.74 -4.74 45.8|HANFORD CITY 1.75 1.65 0.10 106.1 4.32 7.83 -3.51 55.2|BAKERSFIELD 1.27 1.21 0.06 105.0 3.31 5.69 -2.38 58.2|* NORMALS FOR BAKERSFIELD...FRESNO...HANFORD CITY AND MERCED CITY ARE|FOR 1981-2010.|NORMALS FOR HANFORD...MADERA AND MERCED AIRPORTS ARE FOR 2001-2010.

A strong winter storm brought heavy snow to many sections of northern California during this interval.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on numerous reports of heavy snow in adjacent zone CAZ082, it is assumed that Winter Storm Warning criteria were met in zone CAZ083 as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very shallow marine layer created dense fog conditions along the Orange and San Deigo County coasts during the nights and early mornings of the 1st and 2nd. Several coastal airports and beaches reported less than 1/4 mile visibility for hours at a time, some even dropping below 1/8 mile. No aircraft accidents were reported during these times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: John Wayne Airport ASOS reported less than 1/4 mile visibility from 0153 to 0352 PST on the 1st and 1/8 mile visibility from 0553 to 0652 PST and again from 0753 to 0836 PST. The following day, John Wayne reported less than 1/4 mile visibility from 0153 to 0452 PST and 1/16 mile visibility from 0753 to 0830 PST.|Newport and Huntington Beach lifeguards reported visibilities as low as 50 to 100 yards during these times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure over the Great Basin generated another round of strong Santa Ana winds across Southern California. The strongest winds occurred in the mountains and valleys of Ventura and Los Angeles counties with wind gusts as high as 70 MPH reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northeast winds were reported across the Santa Monica mountains. The Malibu Hills RAWS sensor reported wind gusts to 63 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure over the Great Basin generated another round of strong Santa Ana winds across Southern California. The strongest winds occurred in the mountains and valleys of Ventura and Los Angeles counties with wind gusts as high as 70 MPH reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northeast winds were reported across the valleys of Ventura county. The Wiley Ridge RAWS sensor reported wind gusts to 70 MPH.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY SAN FERNANDO VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure over the Great Basin generated another round of strong Santa Ana winds across Southern California. The strongest winds occurred in the mountains and valleys of Ventura and Los Angeles counties with wind gusts as high as 70 MPH reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter in the community of Northridge reported northerly wind gusts to 65 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rip current caused a fatality at Rio Del Mar Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man who was fishing Friday morning at Rio Del Mar Beach was carried out to sea by a Rip Current. The man was taken to a hospital where he was pronounced dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through northern California, bringing several feet of snow to the mountains, significant rainfall to the valley and foothills, and tornadic supercells in the valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lake of the Pines Tornado: Supercells developed behind the cold front along a north-south boundary in the middle of the Central Valley, where both instability and shear were large. The Fire Department reported a tornado touchdown at approximately 4:30pm (PST). The estimated damage path length was approximately 4 miles with wind speeds estimated at 90-100mph. The tornado touched down near highway 49 and Combie Rd and snapped tree trunks of 1 to 3 ft in diameter. Many trees were uprooted and a few power lines were blown over. Some buildings were also badly damaged in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty north to northwest winds developed across Riverside county on the 9th of January, and peak gusts in excess of 35 developed over the lower deserts. The strong winds coupled with very dry conditions led to areas of dense blowing dust. Dust storm conditions were observed west of the lower Colorado River valley during the late morning hours, affecting motorists on Interstate 10 west of Blythe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong, gusty north to northwest winds developed across eastern Riverside county during the morning hours on January 8th. The atmosphere was very dry and the gusty winds, in excess of 35 mph, led to development of dust storm conditions over portions of the county. At noon, fire department and rescue personnel reported dust and sand reducing visibility along Interstate 10 7 miles west of Blythe. Poor visibility was reported westward to Desert Center. In addition, a trained weather spotter reported visibility below one quarter mile 25 miles west of Blythe on Interstate 10, with similar conditions occuring west through Desert Center. Fortunately, no accidents or injuries were reported due to the dense blowing dust and sand.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very shallow marine layer created dense fog conditions along the Orange and San Deigo County coasts during the nights and early mornings of the 1st and 2nd. Several coastal airports and beaches reported less than 1/4 mile visibility for hours at a time, some even dropping below 1/8 mile. No aircraft accidents were reported during these times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lindbergh Field ASOS reported 1/8 mile visibility from 0322 to 0550 PST, from 0624 to 0850 PST and again from 2025 to 2229 PST on the 1st. The following day, Carlsbad Palomar Airport ASOS reported less than 1/4 mile visibility from 2353 PST on the 1st to 0052 PST on the 2nd. During these mornings, Mission Beach lifeguards reported visibilities of 1/8 mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level shortwave trough developed over the Great Basin and moved south and east through Arizona, creating a significant offshore flow event for Southern California from the evening of the 7th through the 8th. Winds of 15 to 25 mph with gusts as high as 82 mph blew through the Inland Empire, Santa Ana Mountains and nearby canyons and passes. For this event, most stations in the San Diego County valleys remained below 50 mph with no damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Fremont Canyon RAWS between 1900 and 2000 PST on the 7th and ended between 1100 and 1200 PST on the 8th. Several gusts over 60 mph were recorded during this time, with a peak gust of 82 mph between 0351 and 0451 PST on the 8th. |High winds began at the Santiago Peak mesonet station between 0030 and 0130 PST and ended between 0745 and 0945 PST on the 8th. During this time, a peak gust of 66 mph was recorded between 0119 and 0300 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a lull in storm activity, a strong cold front brought high winds back to portions of northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bolam RAWS recorded at gust to 58 mph at 16/2203 PST.  The Van Bremmer RAWS recorded  a gust to 59 mph at 16/1831 and 16/2131 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a lull in storm activity, a strong cold front brought high winds back to portions of northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AprsWxNet/CWOP unit at Weed recorded a gust to 58 mph at 16/2050 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The stormy pattern continued as another cold front brought high winds to portions of northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CALTRANS sensor at Weed Airport recorded numerous gusts exceeding 57 mph during this interval.  The peak gust was 71 mph recorded at 20/0655 PST.  The Weed RAWS recorded several high wind gusts.  The peak wind was 63 mph at 20/0148 PST.  The KSIY ASOS recorded a gust to 60 mph at 20/0653 and a gust to 59 mph at 20/0853 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The stormy pattern continued as another cold front brought high winds to portions of northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Timber Mountain RAWS recorded numerous wind gusts exceeding 57 mph during this interval.  The peak gust was 66 mph at 20/1303 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front brought high winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Isolated heavy snow also fell in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A big rig was overturned on Highway 395, several trees and highway signs were blown down, and there were several power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front brought high winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Isolated heavy snow also fell in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer in Baker estimated winds to 60 mph. In addition, high winds knocked out power in the Mountain Pass area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front brought high winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Isolated heavy snow also fell in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several utility poles were knocked over, and a few transformers caught fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass combined with clearing skies to bring freezing temperatures to portions of the southern Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CALTRANS station at North Weed recorded numerous gusts in excess of 58 mph between 12/1815 PST and 13/0130 PST.  The peak gust was 69 mph, recorded at 12/2030 PST.   The CALTRANS station at Weed Airport recorded several gusts greater than 58 mph between 12/0735 PST and 13/0110 PST.  The peak gust was 60 mph at 13/0110 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind this final trough, cold air settled into the San Joaquin Valley. An upper-level ridge built into the state, bringing mostly clear skies and light winds. Radiational cooling resulted in late season frosts over the region during the early morning of February 16th.||An upper-level trough moved through the central California interior on February 18th. This trough brought spotty light showers to the region, and wind gusts to around 40 mph in the Kern County desert. The main impact on the central and southern San Joaquin Valley was another surge of cold air behind the cold front. High temperatures on February 19th were several degrees cooler than the previous day.||A trailing short-wave dropped through California on February 20th. This disturbance triggered a couple of afternoon showers near the Southern Sierra Nevada crest, but otherwise had no impact on the central California interior. Temperatures in most locations around the region were a few degrees warmer as the cold air began to moderate.||An upper-level ridge built toward the California coast on the 22nd, and remained near the state the next several days. Temperatures warmed to above normal on February 22nd, and a few degrees warmer on the 23rd before leveling off the next day as the ridge remained just offshore.||The upper-level ridge moved onshore on February 24th ahead of an approaching upper-level trough. This brought strong warming to the central and southern San Joaquin Valley, with both Bakersfield (81) and Fresno (77) breaking their record high temperatures for the date by one degree. For Fresno, this broke a record that had stood for 116 years, since 1896. For Bakersfield, this was only the second time this year that the high was in the 80s. The other time was January 2nd, when the high was 82 degrees.
An unusually cold air mass combined with clearing skies to bring freezing temperatures to portions of the southern Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bolam RAWS recorded several gusts exceeding 57 mph between 12/2303 PST and 13/0303 PST.  The peak gust was 69 mph at 13/0003 PST.  The Indian Well RAWS recorded several gusts exceeding 57 mph between 12/2301 PST and 13/0301 PST.  The peak gust was 65 mph at 12/2301 PST. The SHF07 Portable RAWS recorded several gusts exceeding 57 mph between 12/2329 PST and 13/0129 PST.  The peak gust was 66 mph at 13/0029 PST. The Van Bremmer RAWS recorded a gust to 59 mph at 12/1945 PST and a gust to 62 mph at 13/0145 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass combined with clearing skies to bring freezing temperatures to portions of the southern Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Timber Mountain RAWS recorded numerous gusts exceeding 57 mph between 12/2103 PST and 13/0703 PST.  The peak gust was 71 mph at 13/0603 PST.  The Blue Mountain RAWS recorded a gust to 59 mph at 13/0427 PST.  The Rush Creek RAWS recorded a gust to 61 mph at 13/0203 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low drew subtropical moisture over the southern half of California beginning November 15th. This warm, moist airmass kept overnight low several degrees above normal. The low at Fresno on the 16th was 58 degrees, tying the record high minimum temperature for the date, last set in 1965. The low at Fresno the next day also was 58 degrees. This broke the record high minimum temperature for November 17th by one degree. The old record had also been set in 1965.||Showers developed over the region on November 17th, as well as a few thunderstorms. One thunderstorm that formed over Madera County lasted for almost three hours as it slowly moved northeast across the county and into the Sierra Nevada foothills. Another thunderstorm dropped heavy rain on Merced County. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, snowfall amounts generally were less than 6 inches, although a couple of sites near the crest reported storm totals around a foot. The cold front that triggered these storms weakened rapidly southward, but sporadic light showers continued across the central California interior into the morning of November 18th.

A weak storm system moved through northern California during the night of December 4th, and moved south the next day bringing a few showers to Merced and Mariposa Counties by noon. Ahead of the storm, patchy dense fog developed in northern Kings County during the early morning hours of the 5th. This was a warm storm, and low temperatures on December 5th were again above normal. The low at the Fresno-Yosemite International Airport on the 5th was 52 degrees, tying the record high minimum temperature for the date. The low at Fresno the next day was even warmed, bottoming out at 55 degrees. This broke the old record high minimum temperature for the 6th by 3 degrees. For the seven days from November 30th through December 6th, Fresno broke or tied the record high minimum temperature on six days.||In the wake of the storm of December 5th-6th, an upper-level ridge formed over the eastern Pacific. The circulation around the ridge set up a northwest flow aloft over California, bringing cooler temperatures. ���Cooler��� was a relative term, however, as temperatures were still above normal.||The upper-level ridge also brought a stable environment to the central California interior. This triggered the development of night and morning fog over parts of the central and southern San Joaquin Valley on December 9th through the 11th.  December 11th also saw the approach of the next Pacific storm system, which reached the central California interior that night. This storm brought up to 15 inches of new snow to the higher elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada, and 3 inches of snow as far south as Frazier Park. Snow levels fell into the upper foothills in the cold airmass behind the front, dropping to around 3500 feet during the afternoon of the 12th.||The storm moved east of the region during the morning of December 13th, leaving only a few showers over the Kern County deserts. Behind the departing trough, another east-Pacific upper- level ridge built into the state, bringing more fog to the central and southern San Joaquin Valley.||The airmass was drier the next morning, and in the absence of fog, radiational cooling allowed lows in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley to drop into the upper 20s to around 30.

The next storm consisted of two components. The first reached California on December 22nd, with only a brief pause in the precipitation before the second component arrived. This was a wet storm, and 3-day rainfall amounts north of Kern County ranged from a third of an inch to around an inch and a third, while the south end of the San Joaquin Valley remained ���rain-shadowed.��� In the Southern Sierra Nevada foothills, rainfall amounts approached 4 inches in the wettest locations, while up to 40 inches of snow fell in the high country of the Southern Sierra Nevada.|The storm moved east of the region on December 24th. With abundant ground moisture and light winds, the stage was set for dense fog development Christmas morning. The fog subsequently gave way to a cold storm that arrived during the afternoon of December 25th, quickly spreading through the region. By daybreak on the 26th, up to 4 inches of snow had fallen on Walker Pass, and secondary roads near Tehachapi were closed due to snow. Precipitation continued through the day on the 26th, and persisted over the mountains into the next morning, with icy conditions reported on Grapevine during the morning of December 27th. New snow accumulations of up to 14 inches fell on the Southern Sierra Nevada, and up to 7 inches fell in the Tehachapi Mountains.||An upper-level short-wave ridge brought another round of dense fog to the central and southern San Joaquin Valley during the morning of December 28th. The ridge gave way to yet another storm, which reached the central California interior during the evening of the 28th and continued into December 29th. Behind the storm, a northwest flow developed over the San Joaquin Valley. Upslope clouds formed along the valley-facing slopes of the Kern County mountains, triggering isolated snow showers that persisted into the morning of December 30th.||An upper-level short-wave dropped into the central California interior late on December 31st. This short-wave triggered a few sprinkles over Merced County shortly before midnight. Otherwise, 2012 ended on a dry note across the Hanford warning/forecast area.||2012 was unseasonably warm with below normal precipitation. Bakersfield had 4.41 inches of rain, or 68.2 percent of its annual average of 6.47 inches. Fresno reported 9.97 inches of rain in 2012, for 86.7 percent of its normal of 11.50 inches.||Fresno had its warmest year on record, with an average temperature of 66.7 degrees, which was 2.3 degrees above the average annual temperature of 64.4 degrees. The last five months of the year ranked in the ���top 10��� for each month. August and September 2012 were the warmest for those months on record, and August 2012 also was the 5th warmest month on record overall.||Although Bakersfield was slightly warmer with an average temperature of 67.0 degrees, this was not enough to rank in the top 10 warmest years. The average annual temperature for Bakersfield is 65.2 degrees.

Behind this final trough, cold air settled into the San Joaquin Valley. An upper-level ridge built into the state, bringing mostly clear skies and light winds. Radiational cooling resulted in late season frosts over the region during the early morning of February 16th.||An upper-level trough moved through the central California interior on February 18th. This trough brought spotty light showers to the region, and wind gusts to around 40 mph in the Kern County desert. High temperatures on February 19th were several degrees cooler than the previous day.||A trailing short-wave dropped through California on February 20th. This disturbance triggered a couple of afternoon showers near the Southern Sierra Nevada crest, but otherwise had no impact on the central California interior. This brought strong warming to the central and southern San Joaquin Valley, with both Bakersfield (81) and Fresno (77) breaking their record high temperatures for the date by one degree. For Fresno, this broke a record that had stood for 116 years, since 1896. For Bakersfield, this was only the second time this year that the high was in the 80s. The other time was January 2nd, when the high was 82 degrees.

Low pressure moved into the Pacific Northwest on the 21st. Only light precipitation fell over northeast California, however winds were strong from early to mid morning on the 21st over portions of Lassen County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 76 and 85 mph were reported in Janesville and along highway 395 near Sears Road (respectively) on the morning of the 21st. The high winds caused the closure of highway 395 from the Nevada state line to Johnstonville. In addition, a power outage and metal roof damage were reported 4 miles ESE Janesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of October 10, thunderstorms developed over the ridge tops of Monterey and San Benito Counties. The storms produced frequent lightning and heavy downpours. A heavy thunderstorm developed near Hollister California and moved west northwest through the southern Santa Clara Valley. The storm briefly became severe near the town of Gilroy and produced large hail and torrential downpours. The storm continued moving west and dissipated in the Santa Cruz Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 3:40 PM an employee of the Santa Clara County OES observed a severe thunderstorm that produced large hail at his home. He measured hail from dime to quarter size that produced minor damage to plants around his home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds to portions of northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Timber Mountain RAWS recorded several high wind gusts during this interval.  The peak gust was 66 mph at 02/0203 PST. The Rush Creek RAWS recorded a gust to 61 mph at 02/0503 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another cold front brought high wind to portions of northern California east of the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Timber Mountain RAWS recorded numerous gusts exceeding 57 mph during this interval.  The peak gust was 66 mph at 02/0203 PST. The Rush Creek RAWS recorded a gust to 61 mph at 02/0503 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low drew subtropical moisture over the southern half of California beginning November 15th. This warm, moist airmass kept overnight low several degrees above normal. The low at Fresno on the 16th was 58 degrees, tying the record high minimum temperature for the date, last set in 1965. The low at Fresno the next day also was 58 degrees. This broke the record high minimum temperature for November 17th by one degree. The old record had also been set in 1965.||Showers developed over the region on November 17th, as well as a few thunderstorms. One thunderstorm that formed over Madera County lasted for almost three hours as it slowly moved northeast across the county and into the Sierra Nevada foothills. Another thunderstorm dropped heavy rain on Merced County. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, snowfall amounts generally were less than 6 inches, although a couple of sites near the crest reported storm totals around a foot. The cold front that triggered these storms weakened rapidly southward, but sporadic light showers continued across the central California interior into the morning of November 18th.

A warm airmass moved into California on December 17th, ahead of the next storm. Lows in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley were several degrees above normal. Fresno���s low on the 17th was 52 degrees, tying the record high minimum temperature for the date.||A potent winter storm reached the central California interior on December 18th. This storm brought strong winds to the region, with gusts to 37 mph in the central San Joaquin Valley and gusts to 64 mph in the Kern County deserts and 71 mph in the Kern County mountains. The strong gusts played havoc on traffic traversing Highway 14 at the south end of Tehachapi Pass, with two ���big rigs��� blown over during the afternoon of the 18th.||The storm was relative dry, however, and snow accumulations were only 9 inches or less, mainly above the 6500 foot level.||The relatively dry airmass allowed for strong cooling for the central and southern San Joaquin Valley once the winds subsided during the evening of December 18th. Temperatures fell into the mid to upper 20s in the coldest Valley locations, bringing the first significant freeze event to the central and southern San Joaquin Valley. Low temperatures the next morning were even colder by a few degrees, with the coldest spots dropping down to 21 degrees.

A strong Pacific storm system brought high winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.  Isolated heavy rain and snow also fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds blew parts of the roofs off two homes and blew over a flag pole in Morongo Valley, and blowing dust reduced visibility to zero and made travel impossible in Landers.  The peak measured gust occurred at Twentynine Palms (KNXP).

CZ_NAME: EASTERN SIERRA SLOPES OF INYO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system brought high winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.  Isolated heavy rain and snow also fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thirteen inches of snow fell in Aspendell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A flat upper-level ridge continued over California through the second week of January, keeping temperatures near to above normal. From January 5th to the 15th, Fresno had 9 days with highs in the mid 60s. Temperatures in the south end of the San Joaquin Valley were less consistent, as episodes of downslope winds warmed highs into the lower to mid 70s.||An upper-level low over the eastern Pacific spun high clouds over California, while at the surface, high pressure dropping into the Great Basin brought an off- shore flow to the region on the 11th-12th. Once again, winds funneling through the passes of the Tehachapi Mountains warmed the south end of the San Joaquin Valley, with temperatures reaching the lower 60s around 1:30 AM. Bakersfield stayed warm all day, reaching a high of 68 degrees on the 12th.||Dry low-level air moved into the San Joaquin Valley on January 16th. With mostly clear skies and light winds, the stage was set for the second significant freeze event of this winter. Low temperatures around the central and southern San Joaquin Valley were mostly in the 20s on the 17th, with a few spots falling into the upper teens. Below freezing temperatures continued through the morning of January 19th, and a few stations dropped to critical temperatures during the morning of the 20th.

A wildfire of unknown cause started at 1055 PST, Monday, Nov. 5, 2012 on the east side of Interstate 15, northeast of the I-15/215 merge, in the Pitman Canyon area. The fire initially burned downwind/downhill in a south southeast direction. A spot fire started on the west side of the freeway and rapidly burned uphill. The Matthews Ranch area was evacuated. No homes were damaged, but a shipping container was destroyed, along with some of the guardrails along I-15. Interstate 15, between the Cajon Pass and the I-15/I-215 merge was closed in both directions all afternoon, reopening on the 6th, except for the slow lanes which remained closed for guardrail repair. Two firefighters sustained unknown injuries while battling the blaze. The fire burned 375 acres before being fully contained at 1830 PST on the 6th.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN SALINAS VALLEY / HOLLISTER VALLEY / CARMEL VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonable heat wave gripped the area on the first two days of October 2012. High temperatures soared well into the 90s and 100s across the area, which was well above normal and resulted in several record high temperatures as well as all-time October record high temperatures. October 1 and 2 had high temperatures of 91 and 93 degrees in San Francisco and 100 and 101 in Salinas. Farm workers who were picking in the fields near Salinas on both days were negatively impacted by the excessive heat, which lead to a fatality of a farm worker on October 2 near Salinas. By the 3rd, cooler weather returned and the heat wave came to an end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A farm worker picking crops in the fields near Salinas, California died as a result of excessive heat and heat stroke which lead to his having a heart attack.

Strong and cold low pressure moved from the Gulf of Alaska on the 28th to the West Coast on the 29th bringing heavy snow and gusty winds to the Sierra and northeast California. Unsettled conditions continued into March 1 as the cold trough slowly moved through the Western CONUS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A widespread snowfall of 5 to 10 inches fell west of highway 395 (locally up to a foot) with up to several inches east of highway 395. Here are some more specific snowfall totals: 3 SSW Susanville (CoCoRAHS) with 12 inches, 8 inches at the Susanville CO-OP, and 5 to 7 inches along highway 70/89 between Blairsden and Sloat. In addition to heavy snowfall, widespread gusty winds of 35-45 mph occurred with local gusts as high as 64 mph along highway 395 south of Susanville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and cold low pressure moved from the Gulf of Alaska on the 28th to the West Coast on the 29th bringing heavy snow and gusty winds to the Sierra and northeast California. Unsettled conditions continued into March 1 as the cold trough slowly moved through the Western CONUS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of snow fell in Cedarville during the morning of the 29th, with localized amounts of 3 to 6 inches reported by spotters a few miles north of Cedarville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A flat upper-level ridge continued over California through the second week of January, keeping temperatures near to above normal. From January 5th to the 15th, Fresno had 9 days with highs in the mid 60s. Once again, winds funneling through the passes of the Tehachapi Mountains warmed the south end of the San Joaquin Valley, with temperatures reaching the lower 60s around 1:30 AM. Bakersfield stayed warm all day, reaching a high of 68 degrees on the 12th.||Dry low-level air moved into the San Joaquin Valley on January 16th. With mostly clear skies and light winds, the stage was set for the second significant freeze event of this winter. Below freezing temperatures continued through the morning of January 19th, and a few stations dropped to critical temperatures during the morning of the 20th.

Clear skies, high humidity just inland from the coast, along with the radiational cooling allowed dense fog to form near the coast during the nights and early mornings of the 23rd and 25th. Drainage flow kept the fog within about 10 miles of the coast, with the exception of the inland, low area of Ramona. Multiple airports reported visibilities less than 1/4 mile for several hours on each of those mornings. The fog created some airport delays as well as security problems for the Border Patrol along the Mexican Border and along the coast up to San Clemente.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: John Wayne Airport ASOS reported visibility of one-quarter mile from 0318 to 0949 PST. Fullerton Airport ASOS reported visibility of one-quarter mile or less from 0253 to 0927 PST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: No reports were received between snow level and 6000 feet.  However, the Sand Flat SNOTEL at 6750 feet reported 11.5 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 29/0800 PST, then another 3.5 inches in 5 hours ending at 29/1300.  Based on this report and the proximity of this SNOTEL to CAZ083, it is assumed that warning criteria were met at the lower elevations in zone CAZ083.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm airmass moved into California on December 17th, ahead of the next storm. Lows in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley were several degrees above normal. Fresno���s low on the 17th was 52 degrees, tying the record high minimum temperature for the date.||A potent winter storm reached the central California interior on December 18th. This storm brought strong winds to the region, with gusts to 37 mph in the central San Joaquin Valley and gusts to 64 mph in the Kern County deserts and 71 mph in the Kern County mountains. The strong gusts played havoc on traffic traversing Highway 14 at the south end of Tehachapi Pass, with two ���big rigs��� blown over during the afternoon of the 18th.||The storm was relative dry, however, and snow accumulations were only 9 inches or less, mainly above the 6500 foot level.||The relatively dry airmass allowed for strong cooling for the central and southern San Joaquin Valley once the winds subsided during the evening of December 18th. Temperatures fell into the mid to upper 20s in the coldest Valley locations, bringing the first significant freeze event to the central and southern San Joaquin Valley. Low temperatures the next morning were even colder by a few degrees, with the coldest spots dropping down to 21 degrees.
A winter storm moved through Santa Barbara county, bringing gusty southeast to south winds to the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sensor at La Cumbre Peak reported a sustained southerly wind of 42 MPH.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure, building into the Great Basin, generated strong north to northeast winds across the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Numerous sites reported sustained winds in excess of 40 MPH and gusts in excess of 70 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northeast winds buffeted the mountains of Los Angeles county. The Camp Nine RAWS sensor reported sustained winds between 40 and 46 MPH as well as gusts between 58 and 78 MPH. The Whitaker Peak RAWS sensor reported wind gusts between 62 and 70 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure, building into the Great Basin, generated strong north to northeast winds across the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Numerous sites reported sustained winds in excess of 40 MPH and gusts in excess of 70 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northeast winds buffeted the mountains of Ventura county. Sustained winds in excess of 40 MPH as well as gusts in excess of 60 MPH were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another incoming front brought high winds to the Shasta Valley in northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CALTRANS Weed Airport sensor recorded a gust to 58 mph at 27/2040 PST.  The CALTRANS North Weed sensor recorded gusts to 58 mph at 27/1300 and 27/1315 PST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong west to northwest winds were reported across the Antelope Valley. The Lancaster ASOS reported sustained winds as high as 47 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A flat upper-level ridge continued over California through the second week of January, keeping temperatures near to above normal. From January 5th to the 15th, Fresno had 9 days with highs in the mid 60s. Temperatures in the south end of the San Joaquin Valley were less consistent, as episodes of downslope winds warmed highs into the lower to mid 70s.||An upper-level low over the eastern Pacific spun high clouds over California, while at the surface, high pressure dropping into the Great Basin brought an off- shore flow to the region on the 11th-12th. Once again, winds funneling through the passes of the Tehachapi Mountains warmed the south end of the San Joaquin Valley, with temperatures reaching the lower 60s around 1:30 AM. Bakersfield stayed warm all day, reaching a high of 68 degrees on the 12th.||Dry low-level air moved into the San Joaquin Valley on January 16th. With mostly clear skies and light winds, the stage was set for the second significant freeze event of this winter. Low temperatures around the central and southern San Joaquin Valley were mostly in the 20s on the 17th, with a few spots falling into the upper teens. Below freezing temperatures continued through the morning of January 19th, and a few stations dropped to critical temperatures during the morning of the 20th.

April began midway through the storm that arrived on the last day of March. As mentioned in the weather summary for March, two-day storm totals (March 31st-April 1st) were between a quarter and a half inch of rain for the central San Joaquin Valley, and between a tenth and a quarter of an inch for the south Valley. The exception was the southwest part of the San Joaquin Valley, which was rain-shadowed and received only a few hundredths of an inch of rain. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, snowfall amounts over the high country generally were between 5 and 9 inches, although a couple of sites in Tulare County had around a foot of new snow. In the western Tehachapi Mountains, cars were stuck in the snow on the Mil Potrero Highway. Gusty southeast winds developed ahead of the cold front, with gusts of 35 to 45 mph in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley, and 58 to 62 mph in the Kern County deserts; a tree was blown down in Mojave.||As the storm moved east of California, an upper-level ridge moved into the state. High temperatures in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley on April 2nd were as much as 7 degrees warmer than on the 1st, and the desert saw even stronger warming in some locations. This warming continued on the 3rd, and then the ridge gave way to an approaching upper-level trough.

A dry cold front moved through interior central California on April 4th. This front triggered gusty winds across the central and southern San Joaquin Valley and over the mountains and deserts. Winds gusted to 45-50 mph over parts of the San Joaquin Valley and the Kern County deserts. Winds also gusted to over 45 mph in the Kern River Canyon, and to 66 mph at Bird Spring Pass in the far south end of the Sierra Nevada.||An unseasonably cold airmass moved into the San Joaquin Valley behind the cold front. Morning low temperatures on April 6th fell as low as 28 degrees, and the lows at Bakersfield and Fresno of 38 degrees were 2 and 3 degrees off the record lows for the date, respectively. Lows during the next morning were only a degree or two warmer.||An upper-level ridge then moved over California, warming temperatures briefly to above normal. Fresno finally had its first 80-degree day of the year on April 8th; the last time Fresno had seen 80 was on October 30th, 2011.
A Pacific low pressure system produced isolated high winds over the Mojave Desert, and brief downslope winds in the lee of the Sierra Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred 4 miles NW of Independence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief downslope wind event in the lee of the Sierra produced a high wind gust in the Owens Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred 4 miles NW of Independence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong low pressure system brought widespread high winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust during this event occurred at the Bishop airport at 1653 PST.  Dust storm conditions closed CA Highway 190 from Highway 395 across the south end of the Owens Dry Lake Bed, where zero visibility was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful, cold and wet Pacific low pressure system moved into southeast California on the 15th. During the evening hours, rain showers transitioned to snow showers, and locally heavy snow fell into the morning hours on February 16th. The snow level fell to near 3000 feet and heavy snow affected much of Joshua Tree National Park. Low visibilities in blowing snow also occurred in portions of the park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful, cold and wet Pacific low pressure system moved into southeast California during the afternoon hours on February 15th, bringing widespread rain showers to Joshua Tree National Park. The rain turned to snow by late evening, and locally heavy snow was observed across the park into the morning hours on February 16th. According to the Joshua Tree NP park ranger, 8 to 10 inches of wet snow fell overnight above 4000 feet. Snow covered many of the park roads and fell all the way to the Lost Horse valley floor. By 6 am Thursday, 8 inches of new snow was estimated 9 miles east-northeast of Lost Horse at Keys View.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season winter-like storm brought fresh snow to the mountains of interior Northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to ten inches of snow fell at the higher elevations as winds gusted to 35 mph along the west slopes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season winter-like storm brought fresh snow to the mountains of interior Northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to twelve inches of snow fell at the higher elevations as winds gusted to 45 mph along the west slopes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems from the Gulf of Alaska continued the strong gusty winds and periods of heavy rain across the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas from March 17 through March 18, 2012.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 0615 hours SR 9 was closed from 0.8 miles south of Felton to 0.4 miles south of Felton due to a downed tree.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds blew a large tree as well as large branches across Old Sherwin Grade Road northwest of Bishop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of enhanced westerly flow triggered sporadic downslope winds in the lee of the Sierra Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 59 mph occurred 4 miles NW Independence at 0442 PST, and a gust of 60 mph occurred at the same station at 1642 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of enhanced westerly flow triggered sporadic downslope winds in the lee of the Sierra Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred 4 miles NW Independence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty winds affected the mountains of Los Angeles and Ventura counties as well as the Antelope Valley and the Santa Clarita Valley. In the mountains, northwest to north winds gusting over 70 MPH in some locations were reported. Across the Antelope Valley, gusty west to northwest winds developed with 50 power poles reported down due to the winds while wind gusts in the Santa Clarita Valley reached 64 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS sensor at Poppy Park reported a west to northwest wind gust of 60 MPH. Along 110th Street between Avenue D and AVenue G, 50 power poles were knocked down by the gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across eastern Riverside county during the morning hours on July 31st. The atmosphere was very moist and unstable, and the presence of a weather disturbance in the area allowed the storms to produce locally heavy rainfall with rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The excessive rains led to episodes of flash flooding, affecting communities such as Desert Center, Mecca and Eagle Mountain. The primary impact of the flash flooding involved road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed across much of eastern Riverside county during the morning hours on July 31st. The airmass was very moist and unstable, and the presence of a weather disturbance resulted in excessive rainfall with rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The heavy rain led to the issuance of a Flash Flood Warning at 1015 am. According to California Department of Highways, or Cal Trans, at 1230 pm highway 177 was closed due to flooding, in the area about 13 miles northeast of Desert Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across eastern Riverside county during the morning hours on July 31st. The atmosphere was very moist and unstable, and the presence of a weather disturbance in the area allowed the storms to produce locally heavy rainfall with rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The excessive rains led to episodes of flash flooding, affecting communities such as Desert Center, Mecca and Eagle Mountain. The primary impact of the flash flooding involved road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed across much of eastern Riverside county during the morning hours on July 31st. The airmass was very moist and unstable, and the presence of a weather disturbance led to excessive rainfall with rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The heavy rain led to episodes of flash flooding, and a Flash Flood Warning was issued for most of eastern Riverside county at 1015 am. According to the California Department of Highways, or Cal Trans, at 1230 pm flash flooding was observed in Box Canyon, and Box Canyon road was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across eastern Riverside county during the morning hours on July 31st. The atmosphere was very moist and unstable, and the presence of a weather disturbance in the area allowed the storms to produce locally heavy rainfall with rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The excessive rains led to episodes of flash flooding, affecting communities such as Desert Center, Mecca and Eagle Mountain. The primary impact of the flash flooding involved road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed across much of eastern Riverside county during the morning hours on July 31st. The airmass was very moist and unstable, and the presence of a weather disturbance led to very heavy rainfall. The excessive rain led to episodes of flash flooding, including the area around Desert Center, and a Flash Flood Warning was issued at 1015 am. According to a trained weather spotter in Desert Center, at 1027 am, flash flooding caused Kaiser Road to be underwater, with flood waters threatening Interstate 10 in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably moist fetch of subtropical air fueled locally heavy rain which caused isolated damage over the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mud, debris, and standing water covered several roads in Death Valley National Park.  A few roads were closed for a day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several dust devils spun across the western Mojave Desert.  One passed directly over the ASOS at Barstow-Daggett Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dust devil passed over the Barstow-Daggett (KDAG) ASOS, generating a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly winds ahead of a cold front helped produce high downslope winds over the Owens Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 82 mph in the Bishop area knocked down a few trees and limbs, and blew shingles off roofs.  A big rig was overturned 10 miles north of Independence, where dense blowing dust was also observed.  A worker at the accident scene was injured during the cleanup process.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds associated with an upper-level low pressure system generated the swell needed to produce high surf along the Orange and San Diego County beaches on the 1st and 2nd. Several offshore buoys reported seas over 10 feet from about mid-morning on the 1st through mid-morning on the 2nd. Seas topped out at 13 feet at the Mission Bay and Point Loma South buoys, 11 feet at the Torrey Pines Outer buoy and 17 feet at the San Clemente Basin buoy. Moderate to severe beach erosion occurred as well as some minor coastal flooding in Orange County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Huntington Beach Lifeguards reported average surf of 8 feet with sets of 10-12 feet. Newport Beach lifeguards reported 6 foot surf with sets to 8 feet. San Clemente lifeguards reported surf of 5-7 feet. Moderate to severe beach erosion also occurred near San Clemente producing a very steep sand escarpment and exposed cobblestones. |Minor coastal flooding also occurred near Seal Beach around the time of high tide on the evening of the 1st. Surf heights of 15 feet were reported then as well. The boardwalk was under 2.5 feet of water at times. Residents reported that water came within a foot of over-topping sand bags placed to protect dwellings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds associated with an upper-level low pressure system generated the swell needed to produce high surf along the Orange and San Diego County beaches on the 1st and 2nd. Several offshore buoys reported seas over 10 feet from about mid-morning on the 1st through mid-morning on the 2nd. Seas topped out at 13 feet at the Mission Bay and Point Loma South buoys, 11 feet at the Torrey Pines Outer buoy and 17 feet at the San Clemente Basin buoy. Moderate to severe beach erosion occurred as well as some minor coastal flooding in Orange County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mission Beach lifeguards reported surf heights of 6 to 8 feet with sets of 10-12 feet at times. Ocean Beach lifeguards reported 8 foot surf with sets to 12 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable air mass was in place over far southeast California on the 13th, and a weather disturbance moving slowly through the area caused scattered to numerous thunderstorms to develop during the morning hours across Imperial county. Additional storms continued to develop and move across much of the county during the afternoon and evening hours, producing locally heavy rainfall and flash flooding. Some locations picked up 1 to 2 inches of rain, and isolated spots saw rainfall in excess of 3 inches. The heavy rain also caused localized flooding and ponding of water that persisted well into the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across northwest Imperial county during the early afternoon hours on July 13th. The storms persisted in the area for several hours and produced locally heavy rainfall in the area to the west of Salton Sea. At 427 pm, the California Highway Patrol reported the closure of State Route 78, west of the junction with highway 86, due to flooding. In addition, there were reports of several vehicles trapped in running washes as well as flooding across other area roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty winds affected the mountains of Los Angeles and Ventura counties as well as the Antelope Valley and the Santa Clarita Valley. In the mountains, northwest to north winds gusting over 70 MPH in some locations were reported. Across the Antelope Valley, gusty west to northwest winds developed with 50 power poles reported down due to the winds while wind gusts in the Santa Clarita Valley reached 64 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS sensor at Saugus reported northwest to north winds gusting between 58 and 64 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather disturbance moving into a very moist and unstable atmosphere led to numerous showers and thunderstorms across Imperial county during the afternoon and evening hours on July 13th. Radar indicated that between one and two inches of rain fell across portions of southwest Imperial county from 215 to 315 pm and a flash flood warning was issued for areas including the communities of Seeley and Plaster City. According to California Emergency Managers, at 6 pm there was a water rescue of an irrigation district employee at the intersection of Drew Road and the railroad tracks approximately one mile west of the town of Seeley.
