The atmosphere was very moist and unstable, and the presence of a weather disturbance in the area allowed the storms to produce locally heavy rainfall with rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The excessive rains led to episodes of flash flooding, affecting communities such as Desert Center, Mecca and Eagle Mountain. The primary impact of the flash flooding involved road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed across much of eastern Riverside county during the morning hours on July 31st. The airmass was very moist and unstable, and the presence of a weather disturance led to excessive rainfall with rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The heavy rain led to episodes of flash flooding over northern portions of the county, and a Flash Flood Warning was issued at 1015 am. According to the California Department of Highways, or Cal Trans, flash flooding caused the road from Twenty Nine Palms eastwward to US Highway 95 to be flooded. The road, highway 62, was closed along the entire length, according to Cal Trans.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoonal moisture rotated around a large area of upper-level high pressure to initiate thunderstorms, mainly over the San Bernardino County mountains and upper deserts. These storms produced very heavy rainfall, leading to flooding in the areas of Wrightwood in the mountains and Victorville in the upper desert. Hail less than one inch was also observed with a few of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Running Springs mesonet station recorded 2.69 inches of rain, the bulk of which fell between 1254 and 1354 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoonal moisture rotated around a large area of upper-level high pressure to initiate thunderstorms, mainly over the San Bernardino County mountains and upper deserts. These storms produced very heavy rainfall, leading to flooding in the areas of Wrightwood in the mountains and Victorville in the upper desert. Hail less than one inch was also observed with a few of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reports roadway flooding along State Route 18 at the junction with Sheep Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoonal moisture rotated around a large area of upper-level high pressure to initiate thunderstorms, mainly over the San Bernardino County mountains and upper deserts. These storms produced very heavy rainfall, leading to flooding in the areas of Wrightwood in the mountains and Victorville in the upper desert. Hail less than one inch was also observed with a few of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm with heavy rain and one-half inch hail began around 1230 PST. Although the spotter only measured 0.68 inches in approximately 2 hours, Swartout Creek is overflowing its banks. Cars are turning around due to flooded roadways, not wanting to cross.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of snow and wind created dangerous avalanche conditions due to the formation of wind slabs on the downwind slopes of ridges around Lake Tahoe. A skier in the backcountry near Alpine Meadows triggered an avalanche.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Sierra Avalanche Center report indicated that three skiers were skiing in the backcountry near Alpine Meadows when one of the skiers triggered an avalanche about 300 ft wide by 500 feet long and one foot deep. The skier was buried under 3 feet of snow. The other two skiers found the victim within several minutes. However, the victim sustained severe injuries due to impact with trees and debris and died in the hospital that night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Chihuahua fire was started by lightning around 1213 PST on the 9th, along Chihuahua Valley Road near Sunshine Summit, northwest of Warner Springs. There were 250 residences and 100 other structures threatened by the fire, leading to mandatory evacuations of the residences and a Boy Scout camp along Chihuahua Valley Road. A hard closure was in place on Chihuahua Valley Road at Highway 79. No structures were destroyed, but two firefighters received unknown injuries. The fire burned a total of 2007 acres before being fully contained around 1730 PST on the 12th.

The Buck fire started around 1156 PST on the 14th due to lightning, along Sage Road between Lewis Valley Road and Buck Road near the town of Sage in Riverside County. Voluntary evacuations were in place. Four residences were destroyed. Five firefighters received minor injuries and one civilian received burn injuries. The fire burned a total of 2681 acres before being fully controlled around 0700 PST on the 20th.

CZ_NAME: IMPERIAL COUNTY EXCEPT THE LOWER COLORADO RIVER VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system brought breezy to windy conditions to Imperial county. The winds started in the afternoon on March 2nd and continued into the early morning hours on the 3rd. Peak westerly wind gusts reached to 50 knots at El Centro but they did not result in any reported damages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system brought windy conditions to Imperial county early on the 3rd of March. The official weather station at El Centro reported a measured west wind gust to 50 knots at 0735Z or 35 minutes past midnight. The high gust resulted in a squall being reported in the special surface observation. No damages were reported with the strong wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Chihuahua fire was started by lightning around 1213 PST on the 9th, along Chihuahua Valley Road near Sunshine Summit, northwest of Warner Springs. There were 250 residences and 100 other structures threatened by the fire, leading to mandatory evacuations of the residences and a Boy Scout camp along Chihuahua Valley Road. A hard closure was in place on Chihuahua Valley Road at Highway 79. No structures were destroyed, but two firefighters received unknown injuries. The fire burned a total of 2007 acres before being fully contained around 1730 PST on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rich Thompson, the IMET at the Chihuahua Fire heard reports of flooding on a few roads in and around the fire on the fire radios. A few crews were stranded temporarily, but no one was injured. He estimates approximately 0.26 to 0.50 inches of rain in less than one hour at the base camp and up to one inch in one hour over the fire itself.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Vallecito Lightning Complex was started by lightning around 1900 PST on the 12th. This complex was composed of 3 larger fires greater than 500 acres and 3 smaller fires of 5 acres or less. The Vallecito Fire burned 519 acres just southeast of Julian. The Stewart Fire burned 10,632 acres near Scissors Crossing northeast of Julian. The Wilson Fire burned 11,669 acres, also near Scissors Crossing northeast of Julian. The Cooper, Wynola and Shoots Fires, together, burned around 7 acres. Evacuations were in place for these areas. No structures were destroyed, but 4 firefighters received unknown injuries. All fires were reported 100% contained on the 19th around 0530 PST after burning a grand total of 22,828 acres in eastern San Diego County.

An upper-level low, coupled with outflow from storms in Arizona resulted in banded showers with embedded thunderstorms over the area the morning of and into the afternoon of the 13th. Embedded thunderstorms produced very heavy rain in the Big Bear and Crestline areas, resulting in minor flash flooding. Later in the afternoon, thunderstorms developed over the San Diego County deserts, producing hail near Ocotillo Wells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported large rocks as well as 4 to 6 inches of water across State Route 18 at Cactus Road.|A nearby rain gauge at Baldwin lake recorded 0.92 inches in one hour between 0400 and 0500 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed through the northwest CONUS and northern NV on February 29th and March 1st bringing strong, gusty winds to Mono County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph were recorded on March 1st. However, winds gusted between 60 - 72 mph at the Mammoth airport (AWOS) between 0730PST and 0900PST. Winds gusted over 100 mph at Mammoth Mountain above 9500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, cold low pressure dropped south from the Gulf of Alaska on the 16th and 17th and brought heavy snow to the Sierra and portions of northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 12 to 16 inches of snow fell in the higher terrain of eastern Plumas and Sierra Counties with this system. Below 5000 feet, little snowfall was noted except in the Feather River Basin along highways 70 and 89. This includes 4 inches in Portola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, cold low pressure dropped south from the Gulf of Alaska on the 16th and 17th and brought heavy snow to the Sierra and portions of northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eighteen to 36 inches of snow fell for ski areas west of highway 89 on the 16th and 17th, with local amounts of 3 to 4 feet near the crest. Elsewhere, the main snowfall fell overnight from the 16th into the 17th with a widespread 8 to 13 inches reported per SNOTEL and CO-OP data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, cold low pressure dropped south from the Gulf of Alaska on the 16th and 17th and brought heavy snow to the Sierra and portions of northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 12 to 20 inches of snow fell west of highway 395 (except 20-24 inches Mammoth Lakes and 39 inches Mammoth Mountain Ski area) from the evening of the 16th through early morning on the 17th. Elsewhere, 10-12 inches fell in Lee Vining with 6 inches for Bodie and Tom's Place. Meanwhile, Bridgeport only saw a very light accumulation (specific amounts missing).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low, coupled with outflow from storms in Arizona resulted in banded showers with embedded thunderstorms over the area the morning of and into the afternoon of the 13th. Embedded thunderstorms produced very heavy rain in the Big Bear and Crestline areas, resulting in minor flash flooding. Later in the afternoon, thunderstorms developed over the San Diego County deserts, producing hail near Ocotillo Wells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marble to nickel sized hail reported from an intense thunderstorm near Ocotillo Wells. Heavy rain and a lot of cloud to ground lightning was also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough in place over Southern California resulted in gusty surface winds in the mountains and deserts, especially through the passes and canyons. Winds were often sustained over 40 mph and gusting at or above 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Sky Valley mesonet station from 2230 PST on the 1st through 0200 PST on the 2nd with a peak gust of 73 mph measured around 0000 PST on the 2nd. The station also recorded high winds between 1444 and 1800 PST on the 3rd with a peak gust of 62 mph. During these times, there were several instances of sustained winds over 40 mph. Sporadic gusts 55-60 mph occurred through around 1700 PST on the 4th. ||High winds occurred at the Whitewater RAWS between approximately 0100 and 0400 PST on the 2nd with a peak gust of 65 mph. Sustained winds 35-40 mph with gusts in the 50s continued through approximately 0200 PST on the 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough moving by to the north of the CWA resulted in gusty westerly winds in the mountains and deserts on the 8th and 9th. The wind-prone canyons and desert slope locations were again the ones with the highest wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred at the Sky Valley mesonet between 1900 PST on the 8th and 2200 PST on the 9th. During this time, several gusts over 60 mph were recorded including a peak gust of 65 mph measured around 1900 PST on the 9th. ||During this time, the Edom Hill mesonet also recorded a peak gust of 58 mph around 2100 PST on the 9th.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Highland fire started around 1220 PST on the 16th, near the intersection of Potrero Road and Wolfskill Extension Truck Trail, near Beaumont. No structures or homes were threatened or evacuated. No injuries were reported. The fire burned a total of 2,171  acres before being fully contained at 1700 PST on the 18th. The cause of the fire is unknown and under investigation.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The View fire started around 2015 PST on the 17th, at the intersection of Desert View and Cottonwood Roads, near the city of Cabazon. No structures or homes were damaged or destroyed. No injuries were reported. Fifteen families within the community were evacuated, but no evacuation shelter was needed. The fire burned a total of 376 acres before being fully contained at 1700 PST on the 18th.

A strong northerly pressure gradient generated strong northerly Sundowner winds across southern sections of Santa Barbara county. Sustained winds between 40 and 49 MPH were reported by several sensors in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds were reported across southern Santa Barbara county. The sensor at Gaviota reported sustained winds between 45 and 49 MPH. The sensor at Las Flores Canyon reported sustained winds of 41 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper-level low pressure system took an inside slider trajectory, dragging a strong cold front through southern California. This resulted in very strong winds in the mountains and deserts as the front moved through and only light precipitation over and west of the mountains. Behind the front, as the low departed off to the east, the winds turned north to northeast affecting the mountain passes, Inland Empire and Santa Ana mountains. Many stations reported gusts over 60 mph, several over 70 mph and even a few above 80 mph. This resulted in areas of blowing dust, flying debris and damage to a few mobile homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Burns Canyon RAWS between 1051 and 1151 PST on the 6th and ended between 1851 and 1951 PST the same day. During that time, several hours of sustained winds over 45 mph occurred with many gusts above 70 mph. A peak gust of 85 mph was measured between 1251 and 1351 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper-level low pressure system took an inside slider trajectory, dragging a strong cold front through southern California. This resulted in very strong winds in the mountains and deserts as the front moved through and only light precipitation over and west of the mountains. Behind the front, as the low departed off to the east, the winds turned north to northeast affecting the mountain passes, Inland Empire and Santa Ana mountains. Many stations reported gusts over 60 mph, several over 70 mph and even a few above 80 mph. This resulted in areas of blowing dust, flying debris and damage to a few mobile homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Johnson Valley reported sustained winds between 38 and 63 mph during the afternoon of the 6th with visibility one-quarter mile or less in blowing dust. Sometime around 1300 PST, a peak gust of 105 mph was measured. No damage noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong northerly pressure gradient generated strong northerly Sundowner winds across southern sections of Santa Barbara county. Sustained winds between 40 and 49 MPH were reported by several sensors in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds were reported across southern Santa Barbara county. The sensor at Gaviota reported sustained winds between 45 and 49 MPH. The sensor at Las Flores Canyon reported sustained winds of 41 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of strong and gusty northwesterly winds affected the mountains of Los Angeles and Ventura counties. The strongest winds were observed around the Interstate 5 corridor with wind gusts as high as 78 MPH reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwesterly winds developed across the Ventura county mountains, near the Interstate 5 corridor. Observations in the area indicated wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH.

CZ_NAME: NORTHEAST SISKIYOU AND NORTHWEST MODOC COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold air mass combined with clearing skies to bring very cold temperatures to northeastern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The only reported low temperature was 30 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold air mass combined with clearing skies to bring very cold temperatures to northeastern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 31 to 32 degrees.

CZ_NAME: NORTHEAST SISKIYOU AND NORTHWEST MODOC COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold air mass combined with clearing skies to bring very cold temperatures to northeastern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The only reported low temperatures was 32 degrees.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY MOUNTAINS EXCLUDING THE SANTA MONICA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of strong and gusty northwesterly winds affected the mountains of Los Angeles and Ventura counties. The strongest winds were observed around the Interstate 5 corridor with wind gusts as high as 78 MPH reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwesterly winds developed across the Los Angeles county mountains, near the Interstate 5 corridor. The RAWS sensor at Whitaker Peak reported wind gusts between 59 and 78 MPH. Other sensors in the area (including Chilao) reported wind gusts between 50 and 55 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper-level low pressure system took an inside slider trajectory, dragging a strong cold front through southern California. This resulted in very strong winds in the mountains and deserts as the front moved through and only light precipitation over and west of the mountains. Behind the front, as the low departed off to the east, the winds turned north to northeast affecting the mountain passes, Inland Empire and Santa Ana mountains. Many stations reported gusts over 60 mph, several over 70 mph and even a few above 80 mph. This resulted in areas of blowing dust, flying debris and damage to a few mobile homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds in the Coachella Valley caused several instances of blowing dust as well as some traffic sign damage and trees down. A street sign was partially knocked down from its supporting pole on Sunrise Way, near Gene Autry Trail. Palm Springs police also closed Gene Autry Trail between Via Escuela and Interstate 10 for much of the day on the 6th to be reopened by 8am the following morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Combined seas and surf built rapidly late on the 17th due to the winds accompanying and following a strong cold front. A reinforcing, large WNW swell arrived on the 18th to further raise surf heights along San Diego and Orange County Beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Huntington Beach lifeguards reported 10-12 foot surf for most of the day on the 18th, subsiding below 6 feet by the morning of the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: April began midway through the storm that arrived on the last day of March. As mentioned in the weather summary for March, two-day storm totals (March 31st-April 1st) were between a quarter and a half inch of rain for the central San Joaquin Valley, and between a tenth and a quarter of an inch for the south Valley. The exception was the southwest part of the San Joaquin Valley, which was rain-shadowed and received only a few hundredths of an inch of rain. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, snowfall amounts over the high country generally were between 5 and 9 inches, although a couple of sites in Tulare County had around a foot of new snow. In the western Tehachapi Mountains, cars were stuck in the snow on the Mil Potrero Highway. Gusty southeast winds developed ahead of the cold front, with gusts of 35 to 45 mph in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley, and 58 to 62 mph in the Kern County deserts; a tree was blown down in Mojave.||As the storm moved east of California, an upper-level ridge moved into the state. High temperatures in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley on April 2nd were as much as 7 degrees warmer than on the 1st, and the desert saw even stronger warming in some locations. This warming continued on the 3rd, and then the ridge gave way to an approaching upper-level trough.
Strong south winds funneled up the Owens Valley ahead of a low pressure system over the eastern Pacific.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred 4 miles NW of Independence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dust devil caused damage in Yucca Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dust devil picked up a tent, throwing it into a power line and shorting out a transformer, knocking out power to 1700 customers.  The partially melted tent then fell onto a car, damaging it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems from the Gulf of Alaska continued the strong gusty winds and periods of heavy rain across the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas from March 17 through March 18, 2012.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 35 year old man crashed his Volkswagon Touareg on the onramp to southbound Highway 1 at State Park Drive about 12:45 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems from the Gulf of Alaska continued the strong gusty winds and periods of heavy rain across the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas from March 17 through March 18, 2012.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds damaged the guest dock at the Martinez Marina. Three boats became unthethered.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems, part of a large Atmospheric River type of pattern, impacted the area during late December 2012. From the 21st through 26th of December, heavy rain, gusty winds, flooding, and mudslides occurred across the Bay Area in these consecutive events. Downed trees, powerlines, and flooded roadways impacted residents over the Christmas holiday season. The event included a tornado touchdown on the coast of Santa Cruz County which brought extensive damage to portions of a greenhouse and flower growing operations. These storms followed a series of significant storms in late November and early December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 30 foot tree fell on Ice Cream Grade, near Winter Creek Road with no major damage and no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another push of monsoon moisture led to more thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. A few storms produced severe weather and/or flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and debris flowed over Kingston Road just north of Excelsior Mine Road as well as in several locations on Excelsior Mine Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another push of monsoon moisture led to more thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. A few storms produced severe weather and/or flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and large debris flowed over Cima Road 3-5 miles south of I-15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The stable weather pattern continued through November 27th. The first in a series of east-Pacific storms approached the northern part of the central California interior that evening. These storms tapped into a long fetch of subtropical moisture, with resulting high snow levels through the first day of December.||While the first storm brought between a tenth and a quarter inch of rain to much of the central and southern San Joaquin Valley���north of Bakersfield���it was the second storm that brought significant rainfall to the central California interior. Fresno had a record rainfall on November 30th of 0.62 inch; the old record was 0.50 inch, set in 1982. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, new snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 18 inches above the 9,000 foot level. The Sierra foothills saw heavy rain, with amounts around 4 inches in some spots.  As a result of the heavy rainfall, some rock and mud slides occurred as the heavy rains weakened the soil.  There were also several reports of flooded roads in Merced, Mariposa, Madera, and Fresno Counties. ||Bakersfield and the far south end of the San Joaquin Valley remain rain shadowed. Meadows Field recorded only 0.02 inch of rain from each of the first two storm, as the storm track stayed mainly north of Kern County.||The combination of clouds and the warm subtropical airmass also kept low temperatures unseasonably warm. Both Bakersfield and Fresno set record high minimum temperatures on the last day of the month. Both cities had above-normal average temperatures, an Fresno had its sixth warmest November on record. This was also the fourth consecutive month that Fresno ranked in the top 10 warmest months. August and September 2012 were the warmest months, respectively, on record, and October 2012 was the eighth warmest October on record. Temperature records for Fresno began on August 16th, 1887.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty four hour rainfall total at Mendota was 0.86 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lasting winter storm occurred during this interval, caused by a series of closely spaced storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter N Tennant reported visibilities of 1/4 mile in snow and blowing snow with winds estimated at 45 mph gusting to 60-70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lasting winter storm occurred during this interval, caused by a series of closely spaced storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 5SSE Yreka reported 21 inches in 24 hours ending at 21/0800 PST, including 9 inches in 8 hours ending at 20/1600 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lasting winter storm occurred during this interval, caused by a series of closely spaced storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 4WNW Lookout reported 14 inches in 14 hours ending at 21/0730 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge was over California at the start of November, creating a stable environment across the central California interior. Fresno tied its record high minimum temperature for the 2nd of 56 degrees, last set in 1941. Areas of fog developed in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley during the morning of November 3rd, but considerably less fog formed the next day.||The ridge remained over California through the 7th, then gave way to an approaching upper-level low. Light showers developed over the Tehachapi Mountains during the late morning of November 8th, as subtropical moisture moved into Kern County and collided with a cold front dropping south through the San Joaquin Valley. This set up a convergence zone over the Tehachapi Mountains that triggered the convection.  ||Heavy snow showers also occurred in the Southern Sierra Nevada, where up to one foot of new snow fell on November 8th.  This was a significant impact to the mountain regions since quite a bit of mountain recreation was still going on at this time.||After this system moved through, As a result, the air remained moisture laden. A weak short-wave ridge moved over California during the night of November 8th-9th, but this was enough to trigger dense fog development over much of Kings County as well as southern Fresno and western Tulare Counties.||Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of November 9th. One of these thunderstorms triggered a weak tornado (rated EF0), that touched down 4 miles north northeast of Tipton at 12:55 PM PST. No damage was reported. This was the 86th tornado to touch down in the WFO Hanford���s seven-county warning/forecast area since records began in the 1950���s, and was the 13th to touch down in Tulare County. Other thunderstorms dropped pea-sized hail on northern Hanford. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, several inches of snow fell above 6000 feet, and a couple of inches of snow fell on the floor of Yosemite Valley.||In the wake of this storm, an unseasonably cold airmass settled over the central California interior. Low temperatures fell below freezing across parts of the central and southern San Joaquin Valley on the morning of November 11th and 12th, and the Kern County deserts had their first hard freeze of the season.
The stormy pattern continued as another cold front brought high winds to portions of northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bolam, Indian Well, and Van Bremmer RAWS all recorded numerous wind gusts exceeding 57 mph during this interval.  The peak gust at Bolam was 69 mph at 20/0903 PST, the peak gust at Indian Well was 69 mph at 20/1101 PST, the peak gust at Van Bremmer was 74 mph at 20/1031 PST. A spotter N Tennant reported sustained winds 45 mph with frequent gusts to 60 mph and occasional gusts to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of significant winter storms impacted the district during late November and early December 2012. The first storm arrived November 28 and then was followed by three more winter storms. Each brought strong and gusty winds, heavy rainfall, high surf, and flooding to the region. The second storm happened on the 1st and 2nd of the month, with the third and final storm on the 5th of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reports Highway flooding along Highway 37.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds near Pearsonville forced the closure of U.S. Highway 395 for a time.

Areas of fog developed in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley during the morning of November 3rd, but considerably less fog formed the next day.||The ridge remained over California through the 7th, then gave way to an approaching upper-level low. Light showers developed over the Tehachapi Mountains during the late morning of November 8th, as subtropical moisture moved into Kern County and collided with a cold front dropping south through the San Joaquin Valley. This set up a convergence zone over the Tehachapi Mountains that triggered the convection.  ||Heavy snow showers also occurred in the Southern Sierra Nevada, where up to one foot of new snow fell on November 8th.  This was a significant impact to the mountain regions since quite a bit of mountain recreation was still going on at this time.||After this system moved through, As a result, the air remained moisture laden. A weak short-wave ridge moved over California during the night of November 8th-9th, but this was enough to trigger dense fog development over much of Kings County as well as southern Fresno and western Tulare Counties.||Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of November 9th. One of these thunderstorms triggered a weak tornado (rated EF0), that touched down 4 miles north northeast of Tipton at 12:55 PM PST. No damage was reported. This was the 86th tornado to touch down in the WFO Hanford���s seven-county warning/forecast area since records began in the 1950���s, and was the 13th to touch down in Tulare County. Other thunderstorms dropped pea-sized hail on northern Hanford. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, several inches of snow fell above 6000 feet, and a couple of inches of snow fell on the floor of Yosemite Valley.||In the wake of this storm, an unseasonably cold airmass settled over the central California interior. Low temperatures fell below freezing across parts of the central and southern San Joaquin Valley on the morning of November 11th and 12th, and the Kern County deserts had their first hard freeze of the season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of significant winter storms impacted the district during late November and early December 2012. The first storm arrived November 28 and then was followed by three more winter storms. Each brought strong and gusty winds, heavy rainfall, high surf, and flooding to the region. The second storm happened on the 1st and 2nd of the month, with the third and final storm on the 5th of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reports street flooding on 8th street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough moved through southern California late on the 23rd into the morning of the 24th, providing another round of light to moderate precipitation, and strong winds in the mountains and desert slopes. Many locations gusted over 50 mph during the morning and early afternoon of the 24th. Another, deeper, trough dug south through the region and on the 26th into the morning of the 27th, bringing more strong, gusty winds to the mountains and desert slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 64 mph was measured at the Sky Valley mesonet station around 1300 PST on the 24th. Winds were gusting over 50 mph between 0943 and 1342 PST.|High winds began at the Whitewater RAWS between 0250 and 0350 PST and ended between 0950 and 1050 PST on the 24th. Winds dropped below high wind for a time during this period, but a peak gust of 60 mph was measured between 0850 and 0950 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled another outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.  Widespread flash flooding and isolated severe weather occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several vehicles were stuck in a flooded wash on Highway 62.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled another outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.  Widespread flash flooding and isolated severe weather occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of water flowed across Gaedke Lane in Wonder Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of significant winter storms impacted the district during late November and early December 2012. The first storm arrived November 28 and then was followed by three more winter storms. Each brought strong and gusty winds, heavy rainfall, high surf, and flooding to the region. During the second storm on November 30, at noon time there were reported 13,800 power outages in the Bay Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS spotter reports 3.50 inches of rain since 700 AM PST with a storm total of 4.88 inches. The spotter observed overwhelmed drainage culverts along the road that were backing up with significant erosion being reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of significant winter storms impacted the district during late November and early December 2012. The first storm arrived November 28 and then was followed by three more winter storms. Each brought strong and gusty winds, heavy rainfall, high surf, and flooding to the region. During the second storm on November 30, at noon time there were reported 13,800 power outages in the Bay Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS spotter reports that rain intensity has increased and the flash flooding is beginning again with overwhelmed drainage culverts and flooding on the roadways with erosion of the banks around culverts observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The stable weather pattern continued through November 27th. The first in a series of east-Pacific storms approached the northern part of the central California interior that evening. These storms tapped into a long fetch of subtropical moisture, with resulting high snow levels through the first day of December.||While the first storm brought between a tenth and a quarter inch of rain to much of the central and southern San Joaquin Valley���north of Bakersfield���it was the second storm that brought significant rainfall to the central California interior. Fresno had a record rainfall on November 30th of 0.62 inch; the old record was 0.50 inch, set in 1982. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, new snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 18 inches above the 9,000 foot level. The Sierra foothills saw heavy rain, with amounts around 4 inches in some spots.  As a result of the heavy rainfall, some rock and mud slides occurred as the heavy rains weakened the soil.  There were also several reports of flooded roads in Merced, Mariposa, Madera, and Fresno Counties. ||Bakersfield and the far south end of the San Joaquin Valley remain rain shadowed. Meadows Field recorded only 0.02 inch of rain from each of the first two storm, as the storm track stayed mainly north of Kern County.||The combination of clouds and the warm subtropical airmass also kept low temperatures unseasonably warm. Both Bakersfield and Fresno set record high minimum temperatures on the last day of the month. Both cities had above-normal average temperatures, an Fresno had its sixth warmest November on record. This was also the fourth consecutive month that Fresno ranked in the top 10 warmest months. August and September 2012 were the warmest months, respectively, on record, and October 2012 was the eighth warmest October on record. Temperature records for Fresno began on August 16th, 1887.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty four hour rainfall total at Wawona was 3.98 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations at Conn Dam measured heavy rainfall. Minor urban and small stream flooding was observed across Napa County due to the heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems, part of a large Atmospheric River type of pattern, impacted the area during late December 2012. From the 21st through 26th of December, heavy rain, gusty winds, flooding, and mudslides occurred across the Bay Area in these consecutive events. Downed trees, powerlines, and flooded roadways impacted residents over the Christmas holiday season. The event included a tornado touchdown on the coast of Santa Cruz County which brought extensive damage to portions of a greenhouse and flower growing operations. These storms followed a series of significant storms in late November and early December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds brought down a power pole and trees in Schellville on Broadway, south of Watermaugh. Broadway was closed for several hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a two car accident during heavy rainfall in Napa County. At 2:25 PM on Saturday the 22nd of December, a car heading southbound on Highway 121 near the Viansa winery lost control on a curve, crossed the center line and was hit broadside by oncoming traffic. The driver was killed at the scene and the passenger sustained significant injuries and was airlifted. The driver of the car in oncoming traffic was also airlifted with significant injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold low pressure system from the Gulf of Alaska moved through California, bringing light to moderate precipitation. A few locations in the San Diego County and San Bernardino County Mountains received between 1 and 2 inches of rain. One of these locations experienced a debris flow along State Route 18, in the upper Waterman Canyon just south of Crestline, where boulders the size of beer kegs washed across both lanes of the highway. Strong and gusty west to northwest winds accompanied this system, with several mountain and desert slope stations reporting sustained winds over 45 mph and gusts to 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Mt. Laguna North mesonet station between 0100 and 0200 PST on the 8th and ended between 0600 and 0700 PST the same day. During this time, winds were sustained 40-60 mph with several gusts over 65 mph. The peak gust of 70 mph was recorded between 0400 and 0500 PST. ||The Volcan Mountain SDGE mesonet station reported sustained winds above 40 mph between 0430 and 0600 PST. A peak gust of 60 mph was recorded between 0500 and 0510 PST.

These storms tapped into a long fetch of subtropical moisture, with resulting high snow levels through the first day of December.||While the first storm brought between a tenth and a quarter inch of rain to much of the central and southern San Joaquin Valley���north of Bakersfield���it was the second storm that brought significant rainfall to the central California interior. Fresno had a record rainfall on November 30th of 0.62 inch; the old record was 0.50 inch, set in 1982. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, new snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 18 inches above the 9,000 foot level. The Sierra foothills saw heavy rain, with amounts around 4 inches in some spots.  As a result of the heavy rainfall, some rock and mud slides occurred as the heavy rains weakened the soil.  There were also several reports of flooded roads in Merced, Mariposa, Madera, and Fresno Counties. ||Bakersfield and the far south end of the San Joaquin Valley remain rain shadowed. Meadows Field recorded only 0.02 inch of rain from each of the first two storm, as the storm track stayed mainly north of Kern County.||The combination of clouds and the warm subtropical airmass also kept low temperatures unseasonably warm. Both Bakersfield and Fresno set record high minimum temperatures on the last day of the month. Both cities had above-normal average temperatures, an Fresno had its sixth warmest November on record. This was also the fourth consecutive month that Fresno ranked in the top 10 warmest months. August and September 2012 were the warmest months, respectively, on record, and October 2012 was the eighth warmest October on record. Temperature records for Fresno began on August 16th, 1887.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty four hour rainfall total at Mariposa was 3.61 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold low pressure system from the Gulf of Alaska moved through California, bringing light to moderate precipitation. A few locations in the San Diego County and San Bernardino County Mountains received between 1 and 2 inches of rain. One of these locations experienced a debris flow along State Route 18, in the upper Waterman Canyon just south of Crestline, where boulders the size of beer kegs washed across both lanes of the highway. Strong and gusty west to northwest winds accompanied this system, with several mountain and desert slope stations reporting sustained winds over 45 mph and gusts to 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Sky Valley mesonet station between 0543 and 0643 PST on the 9th and ended between 0943 and 1043 PST the same day. During this time, winds were sustained 32-56 mph with gusts 56-79 mph. The peak gust of 79 mph was recorded between 0743 and 0843 PST. Another gust to 61 mph was measured later in the afternoon between 1443 and 1543 PST.||High winds began at the Whitewater RAWS between 0450 and 0550 PST on the 9th and ended between 1050 and 1150 PST the same day. During this time, winds were sustained 31-41 mph with gusts over 58-61 mph. The peak gust of 61 mph was recorded between 0650 and 0750 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of significant winter storms impacted the district during late November and early December 2012. The first storm arrived November 28 and then was followed by three more winter storms. Each brought strong and gusty winds, heavy rainfall, high surf, and flooding to the region. CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold, upper-level trough dug south along the West Coast and closed off, before moving inland and stalling over southern California for the 29th and 30th. This system resulted in more precipitation, as well as scattered thunderstorms and a few waterspouts, including one off the coast of Oceanside. Dense fog also formed during the early morning of the 31st, resulting in a large accident involving 4 vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A traffic collision involving 4 vehicles occurred on Interstate 15 near the Lake Street exit, north of Lake Elsinore on the 31st. CHP reported dense fog at the time of the accident. Two vehicles were involved in the initial accident and were blocking the #1 lane. Two additional vehicles struck these vehicles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down across the City of San Francisco. Newspaper reports that public works responded to over 40 downed trees. Blocked roadways at California and Cherry Streets in Laurel Heights were reported. Washington Square, Mount Davidson, and Golden Gate parks reported trees down. Several roads were closed in Golden Gate Park including MLK Drive, 25th Avenue, and 47th Avenue between Fulton Street and JFK Drive. 10 cars were damaged by falling tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two Pacific storm systems brought strong winds to the Mojave Desert, followed by rain and snow. Light snow fell at the lower elevations but did not accumulate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew down a power pole near the Yermo post office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled another outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.  Widespread flash flooding and isolated severe weather occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees and power poles and damaged roofs and windows on a few homes in Trona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level disturbance brushed the northern fringes of the WFO Hanford warning/ forecast area on November 20th-21st. This disturbance brought 0.21 inch of rain to Yosemite Valley, with only around a hundredth as far south as Shaver Lake and into the Southern Sierra Nevada foothills near Mariposa and Oakhurst. On the San Joaquin Valley floor, no measurable rain was reported. The Merced Regional Airport did report a trace of rain, and this was the southern extent of the Valley rain from this storm.||Despite the lack of rainfall from the storm on November 20th-21st on the San Joaquin Valley floor, patchy dense fog developed during the morning of the 22nd. Initially the fog formed in the Los Banos area, but this patch of fog eroded from the north. Later, a larger patch of fog developed in the Reedley-Visalia area, and then spread northward and westward, lingering through much of the morning. At the same time, clouds banked up over the south end of the San Joaquin Valley. The combination of low clouds and fog kept central and south Valley highs in the lower to mid 60s, except for the Madera Regional Airport which only had a high of 58 degrees.||Dense fog redeveloped in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley during the evening of November 22nd, forming a ribbon along the Highway 99 corridor. This narrow band of fog drifted over the Valley floor, causing visibilities at most airports to fluctuate from near zero to a couple of miles. The fog became more widespread toward daybreak, persisting through the late morning of November 23rd.||The 24th saw fog develop later in the night, mainly after midnight. The fog lasted through daybreak, then began to lift by 8 AM in some locations; the fog had completely dissipated by 11 AM.||Widespread dense fog continued through November 27th across most of the San Joaquin Valley.  On the morning of the 27th, dense fog contributed to a chain reaction crash involving two big rigs and one vehicle. Three people were killed in this crash.  The crash occurred on Highway 152 about two miles south of Chowchilla.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems, part of a large Atmospheric River type of pattern, impacted the area during late December 2012. From the 21st through 26th of December, heavy rain, gusty winds, flooding, and mudslides occurred across the Bay Area in these consecutive events. Downed trees, powerlines, and flooded roadways impacted residents over the Christmas holiday season. The event included a tornado touchdown on the coast of Santa Cruz County which brought extensive damage to portions of a greenhouse and flower growing operations. These storms followed a series of significant storms in late November and early December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A massive rock and mud slide near Partinigton Creek blocked US Highway 1 and a private road leading into a Partington Ridge neighborhood. 32 homes were isolated due to the rocks and mud blocking the roadway. Highway 1 was closed for 5 days due to the slide until one lane could be opened again. More than 40 stranded tourists slept in their cars Saturday night December 22nd as they had no way to get out of the area. Repairs took several weeks.

The stable weather pattern continued through November 27th. The first in a series of east-Pacific storms approached the northern part of the central California interior that evening. These storms tapped into a long fetch of subtropical moisture, with resulting high snow levels through the first day of December.||While the first storm brought between a tenth and a quarter inch of rain to much of the central and southern San Joaquin Valley���north of Bakersfield���it was the second storm that brought significant rainfall to the central California interior. Fresno had a record rainfall on November 30th of 0.62 inch; the old record was 0.50 inch, set in 1982. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, new snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 18 inches above the 9,000 foot level. The Sierra foothills saw heavy rain, with amounts around 4 inches in some spots.  As a result of the heavy rainfall, some rock and mud slides occurred as the heavy rains weakened the soil.  There were also several reports of flooded roads in Merced, Mariposa, Madera, and Fresno Counties. ||Bakersfield and the far south end of the San Joaquin Valley remain rain shadowed. Meadows Field recorded only 0.02 inch of rain from each of the first two storm, as the storm track stayed mainly north of Kern County.||The combination of clouds and the warm subtropical airmass also kept low temperatures unseasonably warm. Both Bakersfield and Fresno set record high minimum temperatures on the last day of the month. Both cities had above-normal average temperatures, an Fresno had its sixth warmest November on record. This was also the fourth consecutive month that Fresno ranked in the top 10 warmest months. August and September 2012 were the warmest months, respectively, on record, and October 2012 was the eighth warmest October on record. Temperature records for Fresno began on August 16th, 1887.

A series of significant winter storms impacted the district during late November and early December 2012. The first storm arrived November 28 and then was followed by three more winter storms. Each brought strong and gusty winds, heavy rainfall, high surf, and flooding to the region. During the second storm on November 30, at noon time there were reported 13,800 power outages in the Bay Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 63 MPH was measured at Spring Valley RAWS at elevation 1075 feet. Numerous trees down and some power outages occurred due to the wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of significant winter storms impacted the district during late November and early December 2012. The first storm arrived November 28 and then was followed by three more winter storms. Each brought strong and gusty winds, heavy rainfall, high surf, and flooding to the region. During the second storm on November 30, at noon time there were reported 13,800 power outages in the Bay Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 84 MPH was measured at Los Gatos RAWS at 1842 feet elevation. Numerous trees down and some power outages occurred due to the wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of significant winter storms impacted the district during late November and early December 2012. The first storm arrived November 28 and then was followed by three more winter storms. Each brought strong and gusty winds, heavy rainfall, high surf, and flooding to the region. The second storm happened on the 1st and 2nd of the month, with the third and final storm on the 5th of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down across Smith Ranch Road in San Rafael. Two power poles were snapped off on highway 101 in Terra Linda, closing the northbound offramp to Lucas Valley-Smith Ranch Road. Power lines fell at the Park n Ride lot and across Redwood Boulevard. Smith Ranch Road was closed between Redwood and the Freeway and a stretch of Redwood Boulevard was closed south of Paul Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems, part of a large Atmospheric River type of pattern, impacted the area during late December 2012. From the 21st through 26th of December, heavy rain, gusty winds, flooding, and mudslides occurred across the Bay Area in these consecutive events. Downed trees, powerlines, and flooded roadways impacted residents over the Christmas holiday season. The event included a tornado touchdown on the coast of Santa Cruz County which brought extensive damage to portions of a greenhouse and flower growing operations. These storms followed a series of significant storms in late November and early December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lincoln Street in Watsonville was closed after high winds blew a tree down onto powerlines, all of which fell into the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a mudslide which blocked the southbound lanes of Felton Empire Road, near Bonny Doon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: During heavy rainfall, a 24 inch culvert failed and collapsed, opening up a 20 foot sinkhole on Tarabrook Drive. One lane of traffic could still get by the sinkhole. The Orinda City Council declared a state of emergency due to this large sinkhole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large amount of debris washed onto Emigrant Canyon Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highways 62 and 95 were flooded near Vidal Junction.  A big rig was stuck in the mud eight miles west of the junction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flooded the intersection of Kelbaker and Kelso Cima Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The stable weather pattern continued through November 27th. The first in a series of east-Pacific storms approached the northern part of the central California interior that evening. These storms tapped into a long fetch of subtropical moisture, with resulting high snow levels through the first day of December.||While the first storm brought between a tenth and a quarter inch of rain to much of the central and southern San Joaquin Valley���north of Bakersfield���it was the second storm that brought significant rainfall to the central California interior. Fresno had a record rainfall on November 30th of 0.62 inch; the old record was 0.50 inch, set in 1982. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, new snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 18 inches above the 9,000 foot level. The Sierra foothills saw heavy rain, with amounts around 4 inches in some spots.  As a result of the heavy rainfall, some rock and mud slides occurred as the heavy rains weakened the soil.  There were also several reports of flooded roads in Merced, Mariposa, Madera, and Fresno Counties. ||Bakersfield and the far south end of the San Joaquin Valley remain rain shadowed. Meadows Field recorded only 0.02 inch of rain from each of the first two storm, as the storm track stayed mainly north of Kern County.||The combination of clouds and the warm subtropical airmass also kept low temperatures unseasonably warm. Both Bakersfield and Fresno set record high minimum temperatures on the last day of the month. Both cities had above-normal average temperatures, an Fresno had its sixth warmest November on record. This was also the fourth consecutive month that Fresno ranked in the top 10 warmest months. August and September 2012 were the warmest months, respectively, on record, and October 2012 was the eighth warmest October on record. Temperature records for Fresno began on August 16th, 1887.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a rock and mud slide on Highway 168 about 15 miles northeast of Clovis, which closed the road for several hours while authorities cleaned up the debris.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds brought down a tree on Old La Honda Road in Woodside that closed both lanes of traffic for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold trough dropped along the Pacific Northwest coast on the 8th and into northern California and the interior Northwest on the 9th. The trough brought a narrow band of heavy snow to portions of northeast California on the 8th and 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow developed between highway 36 to the north and just north of Portola to the south. The band extended as far east as Susanville and Janesville along highway 395. Snowfall in this band averaged 5 to 9 inches with up to 18 inches near Janesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure remained over the northeast Pacific from November 29th through December 2nd, bringing high elevation snowfall and heavy lower elevation rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This event brought mostly rain or a mix of rain and snow to the Lake Tahoe area below 7500 feet. Above 7500 feet, 10 to 18 inches of snow fell with 17 to 27 inches above 8500 feet per SNOTEL estimates and Sierra Avalanche Center observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure remained over the northeast Pacific from November 29th through December 2nd, bringing high elevation snowfall and heavy lower elevation rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most snowfall was restricted to above 7500 feet north of Bridgeport and west of highway 395 (near the crest) with this storm. The Mammoth Ranger Station reported 11 inches of snowfall, while SNOTEL estimates above 8500 feet ranged from 12 to 20 inches with local amounts of 24 to 30 inches. The main Sierra passes were already closed for winter so little cross-Sierra traffic impact was noted.

The second storm happened on the 1st and 2nd of the month, with the third and final storm on the 5th of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew a big rig that was eastbound on the Richmond-San Rafael Bridge onto its side. Eastbound lanes of the bridge were blocked for more than five hours with traffic reduced to using a shoulder to pass. Winds continued to be strong through the morning, and crews could not remove the vehicle due to the wind speeds on the bridge. The truck was removed by crews around 10 AM that morning.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding damaged White Hill Middle School and classes were cancelled on Monday December 3rd due to classrooms being flooded. Significant flooding occurred with the library and several other classrooms covered in mud. Two art classrooms and two seventh grade classrooms, as well as an administrative office and a staff room suffered water damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind felled several trees in Napa County. A large oak tree branch punctured the roof of a home on Lake Street in Napa County. A 40 foot fir tree on Cedar Street fell due to high winds and took out a water tank, fencing, and part of a roof and back porch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems, part of a large Atmospheric River type of pattern, impacted the area during late December 2012. From the 21st through 26th of December, heavy rain, gusty winds, flooding, and mudslides occurred across the Bay Area in these consecutive events. Downed trees, powerlines, and flooded roadways impacted residents over the Christmas holiday season. The event included a tornado touchdown on the coast of Santa Cruz County which brought extensive damage to portions of a greenhouse and flower growing operations. These storms followed a series of significant storms in late November and early December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hopper Creek rose to the top of the bridge on Finnell Road, overflowed down Mulberry Street, and flooded the south end of Heather Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and cold storm brought heavy snow and strong winds to Northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Mount Shasta City reported 10.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 19/2000 PST. A spotter 3SSE Mount Shasta City reported 8.0 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 18/1200 PST.  He relayed an unofficial report of 10.0 inches at Dunsmuir.  An observer at Mount Shasta City reported 9.0 inches of snow in four hours between 18/1000 and 18/1400 PST.  A spotter 4N McCloud reported 17.0 inches in 24 hours ending at 19/1500 PST.  An observer ESE Mount Shasta City reported 6.0 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 19/1900 PST with a storm total of 9.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and cold storm brought heavy snow and strong winds to Northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on heavy rain reports at low altitudes during this interval (2.25 inches at Somes Bar RAWS and 0.98 inches at Dutch-Indy RAWS) and reports of heavy snow in adjacent zone CAZ082, it is assumed that heavy snow fell in CAZ080 at this time above the reporting stations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge was over California at the start of November, creating a stable environment across the central California interior. Fresno tied its record high minimum temperature for the 2nd of 56 degrees, last set in 1941. Areas of fog developed in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley during the morning of November 3rd, but considerably less fog formed the next day.||The ridge remained over California through the 7th, then gave way to an approaching upper-level low. Light showers developed over the Tehachapi Mountains during the late morning of November 8th, as subtropical moisture moved into Kern County and collided with a cold front dropping south through the San Joaquin Valley. This set up a convergence zone over the Tehachapi Mountains that triggered the convection.  ||Heavy snow showers also occurred in the Southern Sierra Nevada, where up to one foot of new snow fell on November 8th.  This was a significant impact to the mountain regions since quite a bit of mountain recreation was still going on at this time.||After this system moved through, As a result, the air remained moisture laden. A weak short-wave ridge moved over California during the night of November 8th-9th, but this was enough to trigger dense fog development over much of Kings County as well as southern Fresno and western Tulare Counties.||Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of November 9th. One of these thunderstorms triggered a weak tornado (rated EF0), that touched down 4 miles north northeast of Tipton at 12:55 PM PST. No damage was reported. This was the 86th tornado to touch down in the WFO Hanford���s seven-county warning/forecast area since records began in the 1950���s, and was the 13th to touch down in Tulare County. Other thunderstorms dropped pea-sized hail on northern Hanford. In the Southern Sierra Nevada, several inches of snow fell above 6000 feet, and a couple of inches of snow fell on the floor of Yosemite Valley.||In the wake of this storm, an unseasonably cold airmass settled over the central California interior. Low temperatures fell below freezing across parts of the central and southern San Joaquin Valley on the morning of November 11th and 12th, and the Kern County deserts had their first hard freeze of the season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of strong storms brought heavy snow and high winds to portions of Northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Somes Bar RAWS recorded 1.07 inches of precipitation in 15 hours between 22/0816 and 22/2316 PST. In addition, a member of the public reported 7.0 inches of snow in Mount Shasta City in adjacent zone CAZ082 in 11.25 hours ending at 22/1615 PST. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of strong storms brought heavy snow and high winds to portions of Northern California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported 7.0 inches of snow in Mount Shasta City during this 11.25 hour interval.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold, upper-level trough dug south along the West Coast and closed off, before moving inland and stalling over southern California for the 29th and 30th. This system resulted in more precipitation, as well as scattered thunderstorms and a few waterspouts, including one off the coast of Oceanside. Dense fog also formed during the early morning of the 31st, resulting in a large accident involving 4 vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a funnel cloud over the city of Fallbrook from a thunderstorm nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems, part of a large Atmospheric River type of pattern, impacted the area during late December 2012. From the 21st through 26th of December, heavy rain, gusty winds, flooding, and mudslides occurred across the Bay Area in these consecutive events. Downed trees, powerlines, and flooded roadways impacted residents over the Christmas holiday season. The event included a tornado touchdown on the coast of Santa Cruz County which brought extensive damage to portions of a greenhouse and flower growing operations. These storms followed a series of significant storms in late November and early December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Willow Brook at Penngrove Park gauge reached 62.1 feet. At a level of 60.7 feet, water from the Brook goes out of the banks and flooding begins in Penngrove Park. Above 62.0 feet, Willow Brook will flood downstream of Penngrove Park as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of significant winter storms impacted the district during late November and early December 2012. The first storm arrived November 28 and then was followed by three more winter storms. Each brought strong and gusty winds, heavy rainfall, high surf, and flooding to the region. The second storm happened on the 1st and 2nd of the month, with the third and final storm on the 5th of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water level reached 12.59 feet on San Anselmo Creek gauge. Gauge operators indicated that flooding begins above 11.1 feet on that gauge. Flooding was reported on nearby Sleepy Hollow Creek impacting lower floors of residences on Butterfield Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first round of winter storms hit northern California Saturday, Dec. 15th and continued into Monday evening, Dec. 17th. Snow accumulations were 1/2 to 1 ft for the northern and Sierra mountains, with winds gusting up to 60mph for the mountains. Snow levels ranged 500-1500 ft for Shasta county and above 2000 ft for the Sierra. Heavy rainfall amounts were reported to be up to 1-3 in some areas. Some thunderstorms were reported with small hail in Butte county. This system was significant because it brought snow to the Northern Sacramento Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Waves of precipitation entered northern California, bringing cold air and low snow levels. Trained spotters reported snow in the cities of Redding and Cottonwood at around 500-600 ft elevations, which is a rare event. Only light accumulations were reported...with the most being 3/4 to 1 in Redding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lasting winter storm occurred during this interval, caused by a series of closely spaced storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 1WSW Mount Shasta reported 12 inches of snow in 10.5 hours ending at 20/1330 PST.  A spotter 3SSE Mount Shasta reported 9 inches in 6 hours ending at 20/1500 PST, with 14 inches storm total so far.  The cooperative observer at Mount Shasta City reported 12 inches in 7 hours ending at 20/1500 PST, another 9 inches in 12 hours ending at 21/0700, and another 5 inches overnight ending at 22/0900 PST.  His storm total was 40 inches.  Later on he reported 12 inches in 24 hours ending at 23/2000 PST with 35 inches on the ground.  A spotter SSE Mount Shasta reported 4 inches in 5 hours ending at 20/1300 PST, 6 inches in 3 hours ending at 20/1630 PST, another 5 inches in 2.75 hours ending at 20/1915 PST, and another 7 inches in 12.5 hours ending at 21/0730 PST, for a storm total of 22 inches. A CoCoRahs observer 5.1 NW Mount Shasta City reported 17.1 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 21/1000 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought downslope winds in the lee of the Sierra Nevada and Spring Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downslope winds affected much of the Owens Valley. A power line was blown down in Olancha, and Highway 395 was closed for a time from Lone Pine to Pearsonville. The peak gust was measured 4 miles NW of Independence at 1242 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought isolated heavy snow to the southern Great Basin and isolated high winds to the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred at Bicycle Lake (KBYS).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system and cold front brought scattered high winds to the Mojave Desert and Owens Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: These gusts occurred 4 miles NW of Independence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system brought locally high winds to the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple stations measured gusts over 58 mph in the Owens Valley. The peak gust was 79 mph 5 miles NW of Independence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lasting winter storm occurred during this interval, caused by a series of closely spaced storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter E Fort Jones at 2650 feet reported 14.5 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 21/0800 PST.  A spotter 2NE Seiad Valley reported 4 inches in 21 hours ending at 21/0700 PST, and he reported many power lines and trees down in the area due to heavy wet snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lasting winter storm occurred during this interval, caused by a series of closely spaced storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter N Tennant reported 12 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 22/0730 PST with 22 inches on the ground.  The same spotter reported 5 additional inches of snow in 8 hours ending at 23/0800 PST with 28 inches on the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lasting winter storm occurred during this interval, caused by a series of closely spaced storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter N Tennant reported visibilities of 50 yards in blowing snow with winds estimated at 35 mph gusting to 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm dropped significant snowfall amounts over parts of Northwest California. This resulted in road closures and chain restrictions on the higher elevation roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several spotters reported snowfall meeting warning criteria. Chain restrictions were required on some of the main roads and numerous traffic hazards were reported on these roads.  One of the main roads had to be closed for an hour due to snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface pressure gradient and strong winds aloft led to a short-lived high wind event in the Shasta Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CALTRANS sensor at Weed recorded gusts of 61 to 65 mph during this interval.  The peak gust was 65 mph at 20/1550 PST.  The CALTRANS sensor at Weed Airport recorded a gust to 58 mph at 20/1540 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient and strong winds aloft supported a marginal high wind event in the Shasta Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CALTRANS sensor at Weed recorded gusts to 58 mph at 19/0915 and 19/1350 PST. The CALTRANS sensor at Weed Airport recorded a gust to 58 mph at 19/1540 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge built into California behind the departing trough. Temperatures warmed to near normal by April 16th, then above normal the next day. An upper-level trough moved through the Pacific Northwest on the 18th, briefly flattening the ridge, but the ridge rebounded on April 19th for a sharp warming trend. High temperatures in the central and southern San Joaquin Valley on April 20th were 7-10 degrees warmer than the previous day. Temperatures on the 21st continued to warm, and highs were 4-7 degrees warmer than on the 20th. Both Bakersfield and Fresno had their first 90-degree days of the year, with highs of 94 and 93 degrees, respectively. ||Temperatures warmed even further on the 22nd; record high maximum and minimum temperatures were either tied or broken at both Fresno and Bakersfield.||The ridge began to weaken by the following evening, with an upper level trough over northern California. This trough ushered in cooler marine air into the San Joaquin Valley and much of interior central California so that temperatures cooled several degrees by the 23rd. (This pattern was similar to what occurs during the summer months.) Temperatures mainly reached the 80s on the 23rd and 24th in the San Joaquin Valley; however, they remained as high as the 90s during these days in the Kern County desert areas where there was little influence from the marine air.|Another low pressure trough approached central California by April 25th, bringing strong thunderstorms to the southern San Joaquin Valley, including Bakersfield and Delano during the afternoon. These storms continued to develop and intensify as they moved northward into Tulare County. The main threat these storms produced was heavy rain; this caused street flooding throughout much of the San Joaquin Valley portion in Tulare County, including Visalia, Tulare, and Porterville. Many locations in Tulare County received from around 0.5 to 1.5 inches of rain where the strongest storms developed.||On the 26th, the colder air infiltrated the region behind the upper-level low. A cold air funnel was spotted in a thunderstorm that developed near Exeter, or about 10 miles east-southeast of Visalia, during the afternoon of the 26th. Much cooler air prevailed through the 27th as temperatures were well below seasonal averages. Subsequently, high pressure gradually began to build over the area. Low clouds persisted in the Sierra Nevada foothills until the 28th due to a cool and somewhat moist northwest flow aloft that continued over the area as the upper-level ridge was slow to move inland. Temperatures on April 28th were several degrees warmer than during the previous day and returned to near seasonal averages.||On the afternoon of the 26th, gusty winds developed in the Ridgecrest area.  A trained weather spotter reported blowing dust with brown out conditions.  Winds were estimated at 55 mph and a small shed was blown over at the Ridgecrest fairgrounds. ||The upper-level ridge finally progressed onshore and set up over the region as temperatures warmed back to well above average during the 29th and 30th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough deepened down the California coast and swung through southern California on the 11th, into the early morning of the 12th. This system brought gusty winds and moderate to locally heavy precipitation to areas over and west of the mountains, as well as a few hundredths of an inch of rain to the upper and lower deserts. Precipitation for most areas west of the mountains ranged from one-tenth to one-half on an inch of rain, though Orange County received more rain overall than San Diego and Riverside Counties. Mountain locations received between one-quarter and three-quarters of an inch of rain. Above 6000 feet, a trace to 2 inches of snow was observed in the San Bernardino Mountains. A  couple thunderstorms with damaging winds also occurred near Hemet in Riverside County. ||A deeper upper low developed right behind the last and closed off before diving inland over southern California on the 13th. Instability ahead of the cold front set off several waterspouts and thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 13th. Lightning strikes and wind resulted in roof damage as well as downed power lines trees. The thunderstorms, along with the main area of stratiform rain, brought heavy rainfall amounts over and west of the mountains. Coastal locations received generally between one-third and three-quarters of an inch, locally up to an inch where the heavier thunderstorms passed. Several hundred cloud to ground lightning strikes were observed on the Coastal areas. The valleys of San Diego County and the Inland Empire received between one-third of an inch to 1.5 inches in heavier thunderstorms and along the coastal mountain slopes. Some urban flooding was observed, including a stranded motorist in standing water near Ontario and another truck pulled out of a flooded riverbed near San Bernardino. Some lower mountain locations in San Diego County received up to 2 inches of rainfall. Moderately low snow levels near 4000 feet dropped heavy snow in the mountains as well, especially in the higher elevations of San Bernardino and Riverside Counties. San Diego County received 1 to 5 inches between 4000 and 5000 feet and up to 10 inches near Mt. Laguna. Riverside County was similar with 6 to 10 inches of snow above 5500 feet. San Bernardino County was the big snow winner with several locations reporting 10 to 14 inches above 6000 feet and a max of 16 inches of snow at Arrowbear Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds began at the Whitewater RAWS between 2150 and 2250 PST on the 10th and ended around 0050 PST on the 11th. During that time, sustained winds of 35-40 mph occurred with a peak gust of 64 mph between 2250 and 2350 PST on the 10th. ||The Sky Valley mesonet station also recorded sustained winds of 42-46 mph with a peak gust of 63 mph between 2144 and 2344 PST on the 10th.
